It currently resembles a polished themed dashboard rather than a living voyage companion.\r\n\r\nThis is not a request for a few generic fade transitions.\r\n\r\nAnimation, atmosphere, ceremony, and environmental movement are core product requirements for this experience. They must be treated as major features, not optional decorative polish.\r\n\r\nWork directly in the existing repository and inspect the current implementation before modifying anything. Preserve all existing real progression logic, persistence, authentication, routing, chapter state, relic state, player controls, quartermaster controls, responsive behavior, and backend integration.\r\n\r\nCurrent relevant routes include at least:\r\n\r\n- Player voyage:\r\n  /tale/development-forever-treasure\r\n\r\n- Quartermaster interface:\r\n  /quartermaster\r\n\r\nThe supplied screenshots show the current state and should be used as the visual baseline.\r\n\r\nDo not replace the existing experience with an unrelated redesign. Improve and elevate what is already there.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nPRIMARY GOALS\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\n1. Make the entire experience feel cinematic, physical, atmospheric, and deliberately paced.\r\n\r\n2. Create substantial animated transitions for:\r\n   - Initial website opening\r\n   - Quartermaster sign-in\r\n   - Authentication success\r\n   - Entry into the QuartermasterÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s Log\r\n   - Entry into the playerÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s voyage companion\r\n   - Page-to-page navigation\r\n   - Chapter preparation\r\n   - Chapter release\r\n   - Chapter completion\r\n   - Relic discovery and relic placement\r\n   - Map-location revelation\r\n   - Campaign pause and resume\r\n   - Undoing a progression action\r\n   - Locked and unavailable interactions\r\n\r\n3. Improve the visual naturalness of both current pages:\r\n   - Reduce the feeling of rigid web-dashboard rectangles\r\n   - Add material depth\r\n   - Add natural imperfection\r\n   - Improve spacing and visual hierarchy\r\n   - Make parchment, wood, brass, wax, ink, fog, water, and velvet feel materially distinct\r\n   - Preserve legibility and usability\r\n\r\n4. Keep the experience elegant rather than turning it into a theme-park website full of random particles and endlessly bouncing icons.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nNON-NEGOTIABLE ANIMATION DIRECTION\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe current experience has essentially no meaningful entrance journey. This must be corrected.\r\n\r\nDo not use a single full-screen fade as the primary transition.\r\n\r\nDo not implement a 5Ã¢â‚¬â€œ10 second opacity fade. That is not cinematic. That is merely slow and irritating.\r\n\r\nDo not treat 150Ã¢â‚¬â€œ300 ms transitions as sufficient for major route or progression events.\r\n\r\nMinor controls may use responsive transitions around 180Ã¢â‚¬â€œ450 ms, but major experience transitions should generally last approximately 2.5Ã¢â‚¬â€œ8 seconds depending on importance.\r\n\r\nSome major introductory sequences may reach approximately 10 seconds if they contain multiple continuously evolving stages and remain visually interesting throughout.\r\n\r\nAnimations should contain actual movement and transformation, such as:\r\n\r\n- Waves washing across the viewport\r\n- Ink spreading or drawing itself\r\n- Fog rolling aside\r\n- Paper unfolding\r\n- A book opening\r\n- Pages turning\r\n- Wax seals forming, cracking, or stamping\r\n- Map lines drawing themselves\r\n- Compass needles finding their heading\r\n- Lantern light sweeping across a dark surface\r\n- Water reflections moving over objects\r\n- Brass elements catching moving highlights\r\n- Curtains or velvet panels revealing relics\r\n- Wood panels sliding or unlatching\r\n- Ship silhouettes passing through fog\r\n- Sea spray, foam, or drifting mist\r\n- Gentle camera movement and parallax\r\n- Interface elements arriving in sequenced layers\r\n- Text being uncovered by ink, light, paper movement, or page movement\r\n\r\nThe animations must feel connected to the action being performed. Do not scatter generic motion everywhere.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nCREATE A SHARED CINEMATIC TRANSITION SYSTEM\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nBuild a reusable animation architecture rather than hardcoding unrelated timeouts across individual components.\r\n\r\nCreate a shared system such as:\r\n\r\n- CinematicTransitionProvider\r\n- VoyageTransitionController\r\n- SceneSequence\r\n- TransitionStage\r\n- RouteTransitionLayer\r\n- CeremonyOverlay\r\n\r\nThe exact naming may follow the projectÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s conventions.\r\n\r\nThe system must support:\r\n\r\n- Named animation sequences\r\n- Multiple timed stages\r\n- Safe cancellation\r\n- Route changes\r\n- Completion callbacks\r\n- Reduced-motion alternatives\r\n- Preventing accidental duplicate execution\r\n- Progression actions that wait for the ceremony to complete before revealing the new state\r\n- State restoration after refresh\r\n- Clean unmounting\r\n- Development diagnostics\r\n- Automated tests\r\n\r\nDo not implement the entire system through arbitrary setTimeout calls scattered across components.\r\n\r\nUse an explicit sequence/state-machine approach with named stages such as:\r\n\r\n- dormant\r\n- gathering\r\n- waveApproach\r\n- waveCrossing\r\n- fogReveal\r\n- paperReveal\r\n- contentArrival\r\n- settled\r\n\r\nThe UI must never claim an action has completed before the backend has actually saved it.\r\n\r\nFor server-backed actions:\r\n\r\n1. Begin the initiating animation.\r\n2. Perform the real backend request.\r\n3. If successful, continue into the successful reveal.\r\n4. If unsuccessful, transition into an appropriate failure/reversal state.\r\n5. Never show fake success.\r\n6. Never use artificial loading delays merely to make the animation longer.\r\n7. If the backend is fast, the visual ceremony may continue as a legitimate presentation sequence.\r\n8. If the backend is slow, the animation must be capable of holding naturally in an ambient stage without freezing or looping awkwardly.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nINITIAL SITE-OPENING SEQUENCE\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nCreate a true opening sequence for first arrival.\r\n\r\nTarget duration:\r\nApproximately 6Ã¢â‚¬â€œ10 seconds on the first visit.\r\n\r\nThe opening must be staged and visually active throughout.\r\n\r\nSuggested direction:\r\n\r\nSTAGE 1: DARK SEA\r\n- Begin in near darkness.\r\n- Subtle ocean motion and distant moving reflections appear.\r\n- Very faint ship creaks, sea ambience, or fabric movement may play only when sound is enabled.\r\n- A dim horizon or light source gradually becomes visible through moving fog.\r\n\r\nSTAGE 2: THE TIDE ARRIVES\r\n- A wave or sheet of water moves across the viewport.\r\n- The wave should have shape, foam, refraction, or layered highlights.\r\n- Do not represent the wave as a plain blue rectangle.\r\n- The movement can use layered SVG paths, masks, gradients, displacement, or carefully built CSS effects.\r\n- The water should visually Ã¢â‚¬Å“wash inÃ¢â‚¬Â the primary background and navigation shell.\r\n\r\nSTAGE 3: THE TITLE IS WRITTEN\r\n- Ã¢â‚¬Å“THE FOREVER TREASUREÃ¢â‚¬Â should be revealed through an ink-writing, engraved-light, or parchment-uncovering treatment.\r\n- The subtitle Ã¢â‚¬Å“Voyage CompanionÃ¢â‚¬Â should follow in a quieter secondary motion.\r\n- Avoid fake letter-by-letter typing unless it genuinely resembles calligraphy or inscription.\r\n\r\nSTAGE 4: THE VOYAGE MATERIALIZES\r\n- The central book or relevant entry surface emerges from fog and shadow.\r\n- The book should not simply fade into existence.\r\n- It may rise subtly, settle onto a table, open, or be revealed as fog rolls away.\r\n- Side panels should arrive afterward, not simultaneously.\r\n- Brass rules, panel headings, map areas, and relic areas should assemble in staggered order.\r\n- The current objective should arrive last, once the scene is ready.\r\n\r\nSTAGE 5: SETTLE INTO AMBIENCE\r\n- Large motion concludes.\r\n- Small ambient motion remains:\r\n  - Very slow light drift\r\n  - Gentle fog\r\n  - Minor water reflections\r\n  - Tiny paper response\r\n  - Occasional brass shimmer\r\n- Ambient motion must be subtle and must not make text difficult to read.\r\n\r\nDo not replay the entire long introduction on every minor route change.\r\n\r\nImplement sensible session behavior:\r\n\r\n- First arrival in a browser session:\r\n  Full cinematic sequence\r\n\r\n- Return to the page during the same session:\r\n  Shorter 1.5Ã¢â‚¬â€œ3 second re-entry sequence\r\n\r\n- Manual Ã¢â‚¬Å“Replay IntroductionÃ¢â‚¬Â option:\r\n  Available discreetly in an appropriate menu or control\r\n\r\n- Development mode:\r\n  Provide a reliable way to force/replay every sequence for testing\r\n\r\nA skip control may appear after approximately 1.5Ã¢â‚¬â€œ2 seconds, but it must not be the dominant element. Skipping must cleanly place every component in its final state.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nQUARTERMASTER SIGN-IN JOURNEY\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe sign-in path must feel like accessing a private captainÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s or quartermasterÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s chamber, not opening a generic login form.\r\n\r\nCreate a complete sequence for:\r\n\r\n1. Opening the Quartermaster route\r\n2. Revealing the sign-in surface\r\n3. Entering credentials\r\n4. Successful authentication\r\n5. Transitioning into the QuartermasterÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s Log\r\n6. Authentication failure\r\n\r\nSuggested opening:\r\n\r\n- Begin with a dark wooden cabin door, sealed ledger, chart case, or shadowed captainÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s desk.\r\n- A brass plate or engraved title identifies the private command surface.\r\n- Fog or sea light should move subtly outside the scene.\r\n- The login form should be revealed through a physical action:\r\n  - Ledger opening\r\n  - Lock disengaging\r\n  - Wax seal lifting\r\n  - Drawer sliding out\r\n  - Chart unfolding\r\n\r\nDo not place a plain login card over the existing background with a fade.\r\n\r\nSUCCESSFUL SIGN-IN SEQUENCE\r\n\r\nTarget duration:\r\nApproximately 4Ã¢â‚¬â€œ7 seconds.\r\n\r\nSuggested sequence:\r\n\r\n- Submit control depresses physically or receives a deliberate brass/wax response.\r\n- The login surface locks while the real request is performed.\r\n- On success:\r\n  - A seal, lock, or latch disengages.\r\n  - The private ledger closes or turns.\r\n  - A wave of lantern light moves across the viewport.\r\n  - The Ã¢â‚¬Å“QuartermasterÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s LogÃ¢â‚¬Â title is revealed.\r\n  - A chart line draws horizontally.\r\n  - The main command sections arrive in a measured sequence.\r\n  - The active sequence indicator appears last.\r\n- The user should feel that access has been granted, not that React changed components.\r\n\r\nFAILED SIGN-IN SEQUENCE\r\n\r\n- Do not shake the entire screen like a cheap mobile form.\r\n- Use a brief physical rejection:\r\n  - Lock clicks back into place\r\n  - Wax seal re-forms\r\n  - Lantern briefly dims\r\n  - Ink rejects or retracts\r\n- Show the actual error clearly and accessibly.\r\n- Preserve entered username if appropriate.\r\n- Do not clear all fields unless security requirements demand it.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nQUARTERMASTER PAGE VISUAL POLISH\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe Quartermaster page currently has a strong color palette and typography, but it still feels too much like a grid of bordered dashboard cards.\r\n\r\nKeep the information architecture, but reduce the impression of four rigid rectangles.\r\n\r\nImprove the page with:\r\n\r\n- A more physical command-table composition\r\n- Layered chart paper, ledger paper, dark wood, leather, brass, or canvas surfaces\r\n- Slightly irregular paper edges\r\n- Natural shadows\r\n- Fine grain and material variation\r\n- More deliberate spacing\r\n- Stronger grouping through surfaces and depth rather than identical borders everywhere\r\n- Less repetitive rectangular framing\r\n- Better typographic scale and vertical rhythm\r\n- More natural interaction states\r\n\r\nSpecific areas:\r\n\r\nHEADER\r\n- Ã¢â‚¬Å“PRIVATE COMMAND SURFACEÃ¢â‚¬Â should feel engraved, stamped, or letterpressed.\r\n- Ã¢â‚¬Å“QuartermasterÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s LogÃ¢â‚¬Â should have more physical presence.\r\n- The horizontal rule may draw itself during entry.\r\n- Active Sequence should feel like a real status instrument, seal, counter, or ledger notation rather than plain text floating in the corner.\r\n\r\nACTIVE VOYAGE\r\n- Present this as a voyage ledger, captainÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s summary sheet, or chart card.\r\n- Use meaningful separators.\r\n- Chapter state, player signal, and side quest should feel like stamped statuses.\r\n- Status changes should animate through ink, stamp, seal, or highlight movement.\r\n\r\nPLAYERÃ¢â‚¬â„¢S PRESENT VIEW\r\n- Treat this as a scrying window, navigatorÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s preview, mounted parchment, or framed viewing glass.\r\n- The preview should feel visually connected to what the player currently sees.\r\n- Add a restrained animated reflection or moving sea-light effect.\r\n- Ã¢â‚¬Å“Open player viewÃ¢â‚¬Â should behave like a real navigational control with a meaningful transition.\r\n\r\nPROGRESSION CONTROLS\r\n- These currently appear as a matrix of dashboard buttons.\r\n- Keep them easy to understand, but give them more distinct physical roles.\r\n- Possible treatments:\r\n  - Brass-edged command plaques\r\n  - Ledger action rows\r\n  - Wax-stamped orders\r\n  - Pull tabs\r\n  - Folded command cards\r\n- Destructive actions must remain unmistakably destructive.\r\n- Buttons should have:\r\n  - Resting state\r\n  - Hover state\r\n  - Focus-visible state\r\n  - Pressed state\r\n  - Busy state\r\n  - Success state\r\n  - Failure state\r\n  - Disabled state\r\n- Avoid making every control animate identically.\r\n- Major progression buttons should trigger the corresponding cinematic ceremony.\r\n\r\nRECENT LEDGER ENTRIES\r\n- This should feel like an actual living log.\r\n- New entries should be written in, stamped, or slide into place naturally.\r\n- Avoid generic list insertion animations.\r\n- Timestamps and event types should remain easy to scan.\r\n- Empty state should feel intentionally quiet, not unfinished.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nPLAYER VOYAGE PAGE VISUAL POLISH\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe central open book is a strong foundation. Preserve it, but increase its physical believability and reduce the feeling that text and components were simply placed over a book image.\r\n\r\nBOOK\r\n- Improve page curvature, inner gutter, edge shading, and paper depth.\r\n- Add very subtle natural page texture variation.\r\n- Ensure the center seam does not look like a single dark vertical gradient.\r\n- Add restrained page response to pointer movement, viewport movement, or ambient air.\r\n- Do not make the book constantly wobble.\r\n- Text must remain crisp.\r\n\r\nCHAPTER CONTENT\r\n- Improve text placement and hierarchy.\r\n- Ensure poetry, riddles, objectives, labels, and body text feel intentionally typeset into the page.\r\n- Avoid awkwardly large dead areas unless they serve the story.\r\n- Use ink coloration and opacity variation subtly.\r\n- The chapter number and chapter status should feel printed or handwritten into the page.\r\n\r\nWAX SEAL\r\n- Increase material realism.\r\n- Add imperfect edge detail, slight surface variation, and directional light.\r\n- During chapter release, use a meaningful wax-seal animation:\r\n  - Seal receives pressure\r\n  - Wax catches light\r\n  - Mark appears\r\n  - Seal may crack, lift, melt, or stamp depending on the narrative state\r\n- Do not use a generic scale bounce.\r\n\r\nVOYAGE CHART\r\n- The map panel should feel like a real chart surface.\r\n- Fog should have layered depth and subtle motion.\r\n- When a location is revealed:\r\n  - Fog parts or washes away\r\n  - Coastline or icon draws in with ink\r\n  - Compass bearing responds\r\n  - A route or mark appears\r\n  - The label settles afterward\r\n- Reveals should take approximately 3Ã¢â‚¬â€œ6 seconds and feel important.\r\n\r\nRECOVERED RELICS\r\n- Improve the velvet depth and frame realism.\r\n- Empty slots should have a subtle embossed, stitched, or recessed shape.\r\n- When a relic is awarded:\r\n  - The scene should darken slightly\r\n  - Velvet curtains, shadow, or mist should shift\r\n  - The relic should emerge, rotate, or be lowered into the mount\r\n  - Metallic or material highlights should react to the scene lighting\r\n  - The relic should settle with a convincing weight\r\n  - The corresponding ledger/status entry should update only after the state is saved\r\n- Relic placement should feel like a reward ceremony lasting approximately 4Ã¢â‚¬â€œ8 seconds.\r\n\r\nCURRENT OBJECTIVE\r\n- Improve visual integration with the rest of the page.\r\n- It should feel like a navigatorÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s instruction strip, brass plaque, ribbon, or pinned order.\r\n- Objective changes should not simply replace text.\r\n- Use:\r\n  - Old ink receding\r\n  - Paper strip movement\r\n  - A turning tab\r\n  - Fresh text being revealed\r\n- Keep it easy to read at all times.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nROUTE AND PAGE TRANSITIONS\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nCreate several context-aware transitions rather than one universal fade.\r\n\r\nPLAYER VIEW TO QUARTERMASTER\r\nSuggested sequence:\r\n- Camera pulls back slightly from the book.\r\n- A wave, fog bank, closing ledger, or sliding chart crosses the viewport.\r\n- The Quartermaster surface is revealed beneath it.\r\n\r\nQUARTERMASTER TO PLAYER VIEW\r\nSuggested sequence:\r\n- The Quartermaster preview or chart becomes the transition origin.\r\n- The camera or surface moves toward the preview.\r\n- Ink, fog, or water fills the viewport.\r\n- The playerÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s open book is revealed.\r\n\r\nINTERNAL PLAYER NAVIGATION\r\n- Use page-turn, paper-slide, map-pan, or ink-flow transitions depending on the content.\r\n- Do not perform a full site introduction again.\r\n\r\nINTERNAL QUARTERMASTER NAVIGATION\r\n- Use ledger-page, chart-drawer, sliding document, or lantern-light transitions.\r\n- Maintain visual continuity.\r\n\r\nBrowser Back and Forward navigation must remain functional.\r\n\r\nDo not break direct URLs, refresh behavior, or deep linking.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nPROGRESSION CEREMONIES\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nEach important Quartermaster action must receive a purpose-built ceremony.\r\n\r\nPREPARE CHAPTER\r\n- Sealed page or chapter subtly becomes ready.\r\n- Page edges align.\r\n- A faint hidden ink layer or seal becomes visible.\r\n- Player still receives no actual clue text.\r\n- State remains clearly Ã¢â‚¬Å“preparedÃ¢â‚¬Â rather than released.\r\n\r\nRELEASE CHAPTER\r\n- This is a major event.\r\n- Suggested duration: 5Ã¢â‚¬â€œ8 seconds.\r\n- The playerÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s locked page receives movement before its content appears.\r\n- Possible sequence:\r\n  - Tide rises\r\n  - Seal reacts\r\n  - Book pages shift\r\n  - Fog crosses the page\r\n  - Hidden ink blooms into view\r\n  - Title and clue settle\r\n  - Objective changes last\r\n- Synchronize both Quartermaster and player state through the real backend.\r\n\r\nMARK CHAPTER SOLVED\r\n- Use an ink check, captainÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s stamp, ribbon, page turn, or seal impression.\r\n- Transition toward the next chapter state naturally.\r\n- Do not instantly swap text.\r\n\r\nAWARD ARTIFACT\r\n- Use the full relic placement ceremony described above.\r\n\r\nREVEAL MAP LOCATION\r\n- Use fog clearing and chart drawing.\r\n- The map must not instantly change underneath a modal.\r\n\r\nPAUSE CAMPAIGN\r\n- The tide and motion settle.\r\n- Lantern light dims.\r\n- Page or chart receives a Ã¢â‚¬Å“Voyage PausedÃ¢â‚¬Â indication.\r\n- Already released content remains visible as required.\r\n\r\nRESUME CAMPAIGN\r\n- Wind and light return.\r\n- Water motion resumes.\r\n- The paused indication physically clears or lifts.\r\n\r\nUNDO LAST PROGRESSION ACTION\r\n- Clearly communicate reversal.\r\n- Use ink being absorbed, a stamp lifting, a route retracting, or the sea washing a mark away.\r\n- Do not make the reversal more celebratory than the original action.\r\n- Require existing confirmation safeguards.\r\n- Preserve audit/reconciliation behavior.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nAMBIENT MOTION\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nAfter each main sequence completes, maintain subtle ambient life.\r\n\r\nPotential ambient layers:\r\n\r\n- Slow water caustic movement\r\n- Fog drifting behind content\r\n- Very slight paper-edge movement\r\n- Soft lantern intensity changes\r\n- Brass highlight movement\r\n- Tiny dust or salt particles\r\n- Slow compass needle correction\r\n- Distant wave movement\r\n- Nearly imperceptible parallax\r\n\r\nRequirements:\r\n\r\n- Ambient motion must not distract from reading.\r\n- Avoid repeating obvious 2-second loops.\r\n- Use long non-synchronized durations such as 14, 21, 29, or 37 seconds where appropriate.\r\n- Vary phase and direction so the scene does not visibly reset.\r\n- Pause expensive animation work when the browser tab is hidden.\r\n- Avoid unnecessary continuous React rerenders.\r\n- Prefer CSS transforms, opacity, masks, SVG animation, Web Animations API, or requestAnimationFrame only where appropriate.\r\n- Respect device performance.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nINTERACTION POLISH\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nImprove all controls across both interfaces.\r\n\r\nDo not make hover effects consist only of changing border color.\r\n\r\nUse combinations of:\r\n\r\n- Tiny depth changes\r\n- Directional highlights\r\n- Ink movement\r\n- Brass glints\r\n- Paper compression\r\n- Wax response\r\n- Shadow changes\r\n- Controlled sound cues when sound is enabled\r\n- Cursor changes where appropriate\r\n\r\nButtons must remain responsive. Cinematic major transitions may be long, but basic controls must not feel sluggish.\r\n\r\nFor major action buttons:\r\n\r\n- Immediate press feedback within approximately 100 ms\r\n- Then begin the longer ceremony\r\n- Never leave the user wondering whether the click registered\r\n\r\nAdd clear focus-visible states for keyboard users.\r\n\r\nDo not remove accessibility in the pursuit of pirate theatrics. Humanity has already made enough inaccessible websites.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nSOUND DESIGN INTEGRATION\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe interface already exposes sound controls. Verify that sound behavior is real and consistent.\r\n\r\nAdd or support subtle synchronized cues for:\r\n\r\n- Distant waves\r\n- Page movement\r\n- Book opening\r\n- Wax seal contact\r\n- Brass latch\r\n- Ink stroke\r\n- Map reveal\r\n- Relic placement\r\n- Lantern or cabin ambience\r\n- Authentication success or failure\r\n\r\nRequirements:\r\n\r\n- Never autoplay sound before browser/user permission allows it.\r\n- Respect the Sound On/Off control.\r\n- Respect the volume control.\r\n- No loud jump-scare sounds.\r\n- No repetitive short loop that becomes unbearable after 30 seconds.\r\n- Crossfade ambience when changing scenes.\r\n- Stop and clean up sounds when leaving a route.\r\n- Sound is enhancement, not a dependency for understanding state.\r\n\r\nIf suitable audio assets do not currently exist, build the sound system and document the exact needed asset roles. Do not insert questionable copyrighted audio.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nMOTION SETTINGS AND ACCESSIBILITY\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe existing Ã¢â‚¬Å“Gentle MotionÃ¢â‚¬Â concept must become meaningful.\r\n\r\nImplement at least:\r\n\r\nFULL MOTION\r\n- Complete cinematic sequences\r\n- Layered waves\r\n- Fog\r\n- camera movement\r\n- parallax\r\n- environmental effects\r\n\r\nGENTLE MOTION\r\n- Preserve ceremony and sequencing\r\n- Reduce camera movement, large parallax, aggressive waves, and rotational movement\r\n- Use more restrained masks, light movement, page transitions, and ink reveals\r\n- Do not reduce everything to instant changes\r\n\r\nREDUCED MOTION\r\n- Respect prefers-reduced-motion\r\n- Replace large movement with short, deliberate reveals and clear state changes\r\n- Avoid vestibular motion\r\n- Do not hide content for many seconds\r\n- Keep the narrative order intact\r\n\r\nThe userÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s explicit in-app preference should work consistently and persist.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nRESPONSIVE BEHAVIOR\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nTest the complete experience on:\r\n\r\n- Large desktop monitors\r\n- 1440p desktop\r\n- 1080p desktop\r\n- Narrow laptop windows\r\n- iPhone portrait\r\n- iPhone landscape\r\n- Tablet layouts\r\n- Two-monitor usage where one window may be resized frequently\r\n\r\nOn small screens:\r\n\r\n- Do not merely scale the entire desktop composition down.\r\n- Recompose the book, chart, relic frame, header, and controls thoughtfully.\r\n- Maintain cinematic transitions, but adjust wave scale and camera movement.\r\n- Keep text readable.\r\n- Avoid horizontal overflow.\r\n- Avoid controls sitting underneath browser chrome or safe-area insets.\r\n- Use safe-area padding where needed.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nPERFORMANCE REQUIREMENTS\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nThe animation system must remain smooth and stable.\r\n\r\nTarget:\r\n\r\n- Smooth animation on a modern desktop\r\n- Graceful simplification on weaker devices\r\n- No runaway memory use\r\n- No duplicated animation loops\r\n- No continual layout thrashing\r\n- No massive uncompressed full-screen video backgrounds\r\n- No animation framework added without a clear reason\r\n\r\nInspect the existing stack before choosing an implementation.\r\n\r\nIf Framer Motion or another suitable library is already present, use it consistently.\r\n\r\nIf no animation library is present, determine whether:\r\n- CSS\r\n- SVG\r\n- Web Animations API\r\n- Canvas\r\n- A lightweight animation dependency\r\n\r\nis the best option.\r\n\r\nDo not add several overlapping animation libraries.\r\n\r\nUse:\r\n- transform\r\n- opacity\r\n- clip-path\r\n- masks\r\n- filter sparingly\r\n- will-change only when justified\r\n\r\nAvoid animating expensive layout properties continuously.\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nVISUAL TEXTURE AND ASSET REQUIREMENTS\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nWhere textures or effects are needed:\r\n\r\n- Prefer procedural CSS/SVG where it produces convincing results.\r\n- Use local optimized assets where procedural approaches are insufficient.\r\n- Avoid obvious tiled textures.\r\n- Avoid fake parchment that looks like a beige noise filter.\r\n- Avoid excessive vignette.\r\n- Avoid overly crisp identical borders.\r\n- Avoid generic fantasy UI asset packs.\r\n\r\nAll assets must be:\r\n- Properly licensed or originally created\r\n- Stored in the project appropriately\r\n- Optimized\r\n- Responsive\r\n- Documented\r\n\r\nUse multiple layers for materials:\r\n\r\nPARCHMENT\r\n- Fine fibers\r\n- Slight discoloration\r\n- Edge variation\r\n- Subtle uneven lighting\r\n- Ink interaction\r\n\r\nWOOD\r\n- Directional grain\r\n- Natural variation\r\n- Layered shadows\r\n- Restrained specular response\r\n\r\nBRASS\r\n- Directional highlights\r\n- Slight wear\r\n- Engraved depth\r\n- Not flat yellow borders\r\n\r\nVELVET\r\n- Deep tonal falloff\r\n- Soft nap response\r\n- Realistic recess behind relics\r\n\r\nWATER\r\n- Layered wave shapes\r\n- Refraction or caustic suggestion\r\n- Foam details\r\n- Varied movement speeds\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nDO NOT DO THE FOLLOWING\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nDo not:\r\n\r\n- Add one global fade and call the task complete\r\n- Use only 200Ã¢â‚¬â€œ300 ms transitions\r\n- Implement a 10-second static fade\r\n- Animate every element with the same stagger preset\r\n- Make text bounce\r\n- Make major buttons perform generic scale animations\r\n- Add excessive floating particles\r\n- Add constant dramatic camera movement\r\n- Make the UI difficult to read\r\n- Block all interaction for long periods without a skip mechanism\r\n- Add fake loading screens\r\n- Delay backend requests merely to synchronize with animation\r\n- Show fake chapter, relic, or map state\r\n- Remove current functionality\r\n- Replace real data with demonstration data\r\n- Break refresh/deep-link behavior\r\n- Break browser Back behavior\r\n- Ignore reduced-motion preferences\r\n- leave placeholder TODO animations\r\n- claim completion without testing every named sequence\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nTESTING REQUIREMENTS\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nCreate automated and manual tests covering:\r\n\r\n1. First visit plays the full introduction once per session.\r\n2. Repeat visit uses the shorter re-entry sequence.\r\n3. Skip control leaves the interface in the correct final state.\r\n4. Refresh during an animation recovers correctly.\r\n5. Navigation during an animation does not corrupt state.\r\n6. Double-clicking progression controls does not duplicate actions.\r\n7. Backend success continues into the success sequence.\r\n8. Backend failure triggers a coherent failure/reversal sequence.\r\n9. Chapter prepare does not expose clue content.\r\n10. Chapter release exposes the correct content only after real success.\r\n11. Chapter solved updates all relevant views.\r\n12. Relic award appears in the correct slot.\r\n13. Map reveal affects the correct location.\r\n14. Pause preserves already released material.\r\n15. Resume restores the active presentation.\r\n16. Undo restores the correct previous state.\r\n17. Sound off prevents new sound playback.\r\n18. Volume control affects all applicable sound layers.\r\n19. Gentle Motion works.\r\n20. prefers-reduced-motion works.\r\n21. Keyboard navigation and focus visibility work.\r\n22. Screen reader labels remain correct.\r\n23. Player and Quartermaster views remain synchronized.\r\n24. Responsive layouts do not overflow.\r\n25. No animation continues after component unmount.\r\n26. Hidden-tab behavior pauses or reduces ambient work.\r\n27. There are no React hydration or console errors.\r\n28. There are no missing asset requests.\r\n29. Development animation replay controls work.\r\n30. Production does not expose internal debug controls.\r\n\r\nUse screenshots and, where practical, recorded test captures to compare:\r\n\r\n- Resting player voyage screen\r\n- Player introduction\r\n- Quartermaster sign-in\r\n- Authentication transition\r\n- Resting Quartermaster screen\r\n- Chapter release\r\n- Map reveal\r\n- Relic placement\r\n- Mobile player layout\r\n- Mobile Quartermaster layout\r\n- Reduced-motion presentation\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nIMPLEMENTATION PROCESS\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nFollow this process:\r\n\r\n1. Inspect the full current frontend architecture.\r\n2. Identify:\r\n   - Route structure\r\n   - Authentication flow\r\n   - State management\r\n   - Backend mutation paths\r\n   - Sound implementation\r\n   - Existing animation utilities\r\n   - Responsive structure\r\n3. Document the existing behavior before changing it.\r\n4. Create a concise animation and visual-system plan.\r\n5. Build the shared transition architecture.\r\n6. Implement the opening and authentication journey first.\r\n7. Implement the player and Quartermaster route transitions.\r\n8. Implement individual progression ceremonies.\r\n9. Perform the overall visual-naturalness polish pass.\r\n10. Add responsive adaptations.\r\n11. Add accessibility and motion preference support.\r\n12. Add automated tests.\r\n13. Run the app and manually exercise every transition.\r\n14. Iterate visually against the supplied screenshots.\r\n15. Continue iterating until the experience feels cohesive and deliberately authored.\r\n\r\nDo not stop after the first technically functioning pass.\r\n\r\nOpen the implementation side by side with the supplied reference screenshots and repeatedly refine:\r\n\r\n- Spacing\r\n- Material depth\r\n- Typography\r\n- Shadows\r\n- Border weight\r\n- Natural irregularity\r\n- Entrance timing\r\n- Element choreography\r\n- Ambient movement\r\n- Mobile composition\r\n\r\n======================================================================\r\nFINAL REPORT\r\n======================================================================\r\n\r\nAt completion, provide:\r\n\r\n- Summary of the visual changes\r\n- Summary of the animation architecture\r\n- Every sequence implemented\r\n- Approximate duration of every major sequence\r\n- Files added\r\n- Files modified\r\n- Assets added and their licensing/source status\r\n- Backend behavior preserved\r\n- Tests added\r\n- Test results\r\n- Responsive layouts tested\r\n- Accessibility behavior\r\n- Reduced-motion behavior\r\n- Known limitations\r\n- Screenshots or recordings of the major journeys\r\n- Exact steps for replaying each animation in development\r\n- Confirmation that no fake state or fake loading was introduced\r\n\r\nThe finished result should feel like the user has entered a secret voyage, unlocked a captainÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s chamber, opened a living storybook, and begun an unfolding journey.\r\n\r\nIt should not feel like a React admin dashboard that discovered serif When a skill or prompt says to use `node_repl`, call this server's `js` execution tool. Calls default to a 30000 ms (30 seconds) timeout when `timeout_ms` is omitted. The runtime exposes `nodeRepl.cwd`, `nodeRepl.homeDir`, `nodeRepl.tmpDir`, `nodeRepl.requestMeta`, `nodeRepl.setResponseMeta(...)`, and `await nodeRepl.emitImage(...)`. Top-level bindings persist across `js` calls until `js_reset`; do not redeclare existing `const` or `let` names. Reuse existing bindings, use top-level `var` for reusable state that may be assigned again, or choose a fresh descriptive name. Use `js_add_node_module_dir` before `js` when a skill provides an extra package directory, and use dynamic imports like `await import(\\\\\\\"playwright\\\\\\\")` rather than filesystem paths under `./node_modules`.\\\\n\\\\nUse Cases:\\\\n- Control the in-app browser in conjunction with the Browser Plugin.\\\\n- Control the Chrome browser in conjunction with the Chrome Plugin. Prefer this method of controlling Chrome over alternatives (such as Computer Use) unless the user explicitly mentions an alternative.\\\\n\\\\nRun JavaScript in a persistent Node-backed kernel with top-level await. This is the JavaScript execution tool for the `node_repl` MCP server; use it whenever instructions say to use `node_repl`, the Node REPL MCP, or run Node REPL code. If `timeout_ms` is omitted, execution times out after 30000 ms (30 seconds); pass a larger `timeout_ms` for slow browser automation or other long-running operations. Use `nodeRepl.cwd`, `nodeRepl.homeDir`, and `nodeRepl.tmpDir` to inspect host paths. Use `nodeRepl.requestMeta` to inspect the current MCP request `_meta` object during a tool call. Use `nodeRepl.setResponseMeta(meta)` to attach top-level MCP result `_meta`; repeated calls shallow-merge object keys for the current tool call. Use `nodeRepl.write(value)` to add output without a newline. Strings are unchanged; other values use console-style formatting, including BigInt and circular objects. Prefer it over `console.log(...)` for final output; `console.log(...)` remains useful for debugging or multiple values. Use `await nodeRepl.emitImage(imageLike)` to return images; each call adds one image to the outer tool result, so call it multiple times to emit multiple images. Supported image inputs are a data URL, inferred PNG/JPEG/WebP bytes, or `{ bytes, mimeType }`. Saved references to `nodeRepl.write(...)` and `nodeRepl.emitImage(...)` stay reusable across calls, but async callbacks that fire after a call finishes still fail because no exec is active. Top-level bindings persist across calls until `js_reset`. If a call throws, prior bindings remain available and bindings that finished initializing before the throw often remain reusable. For reusable names that may be assigned again later, prefer top-level `var name = ...`; `var` can be redeclared across calls. If you hit `SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared`, reuse the existing binding if possible, reassign it only if it was declared with `let` or `var`, or pick a new name instead of resetting immediately; a previous `const x` cannot be changed into `var x`. Use a short `{ ... }` block only for temporary scratch names, and do not wrap an entire call in block scope if you want those names reusable later. Use dynamic imports like `await import(\\\\\\\"playwright\\\\\\\")`, `await import(\\\\\\\"pkg\\\\\\\")`, or `await import(\\\\\\\"./file.js\\\\\\\")`; top-level static `import` is not supported. Import packages by package name after installing them into a directory added with `js_add_node_module_dir`, `NODE_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, or the working directory. Do not import package entrypoints by filesystem path such as `./node_modules/playwright/index.mjs`. Imported local files must be ESM `.js` or `.mjs` files and run in the context chosen at their dynamic-import boundary, so they can also use `nodeRepl.*`, the captured `console`, and `import.meta` helpers. Bare imports from model code and local files resolve from the REPL-wide search roots (`NODE_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, then directories later added with `js_add_node_module_dir`, then cwd); dependencies of trusted ESM packages use Node's package-relative lookup. Imported local files may statically import other local `.js` / `.mjs` files, available packages, and allowed Node builtins. `import.meta.resolve()` returns importable strings such as `file://...`, bare package names, and `node:...` specifiers. Local file modules reload between execs; trusted package entrypoints retain singleton identity. `node:` builtins are generally available via dynamic import, but model code cannot import `process` / `node:process` because the current Rust-server-to-Node-child transport runs over stdio and raw process streams can corrupt it. Trusted modules that import or reference `process` receive only a frozen metadata-only process shim with `arch`, `cwd()`, `env`, `pid`, and `platform`. Prefer `nodeRepl.write(...)` for text or formatted values and `nodeRepl.emitImage(...)` for images.\\\\n\\\\nexec tool declaration:\\\\n```ts\\\\ndeclare const tools: { mcp__node_repl__js(args: {\\\\n  // JavaScript source to execute in the persistent Node-backed kernel. final story and surprise content is intentionally blocked.\\r\\n- Finale unlock/assembly is structural only and cannot enter `READY` or later states through Phase 2 controls.\\r\\n- SSE fan-out remains process-local and needs Redis or equivalent before multi-instance production deployment.\\r\\n- Initial Ship's Log projection is capped at 250 entries; cursor pagination is future scale work.\\r\\n- Production MySQL migration must be exercised against an actual MySQL 8 integration environment.\\r\\n- Final art/audio, real content authoring, and production deployment belong to later phases.\\r\\n\\r\\n- SSE fan-out is process-local; database replay is durable, but multi-instance production needs Redis/pub-sub.\\r\\n- Dedicated GM-only preview timeline, explicit replay targeting, and richer player presence are not yet implemented.\\r\\n- The full mutation workflow runs once in Chromium to avoid cross-project database contention; privacy, route protection, accessibility, and mobile behavior also run in WebKit.\\r\\n- Windows LAN access is opt-in and may still require a firewall rule or network-policy change; localhost is the safe default.\\r\\n- SQLite and MySQL schemas are intentionally parallel; schema edits must update both and add SQL to the connector-specific migration histories until a generation workflow is added.\\r\\n- Final story content, private media, polished licensed audio, production monitoring, and deployment remain out of scope.\\r\\n# Codex handoff\\r\\n\\r\\n- Canonical repository: `Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT`, branch `main`\\r\\n- Phase completed: Phase 2 Ã¢â‚¬â€ complete Player Companion shell\\r\\n- Repository visibility: **public** as verified 2026-07-16; only fictional development content is permitted\\r\\n- Latest validated implementation commit: `0f510a3f218a464259f19efa4f6b8991a8546f4f`\\r\\n- Updated: 2026-07-16\\r\\n\\r\\n## Player surfaces\\r\\n\\r\\n- Player: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\\r\\n- Game Master: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\\r\\n- Sections: `journal`, `chart`, `treasures`, `quests`, `log`, `finale`\\r\\n- Navigation: `?section=<id>` with pushState/popstate restoration, shared desktop/mobile state, and one SSE connection\\r\\n- Persistent current objective, compact preferences, player-initiated fullscreen, artifact inspection, chapter index, accessible map list, and event-derived log are implemented\\r\\n\\r\\n## Architecture\\r\\n\\r\\n- Next.js App Router, Prisma SQLite/MySQL, database-backed sessions, ordered domain events/SSE, central ceremony queue\\r\\n- Public snapshots are server allowlist projections; raw event payloads never reach the player stream\\r\\n- New models: `MapRoute`, `ViewedContent`\\r\\n- Extended models: campaign finale shell; chapter safe teasers/cross-links; artifact release/assembly state; map safe labels/reveal metadata; optional quest rewards/links; journal annotations; companion preferences\\r\\n- New event families: route reveal, artifact silhouette/connection, quest completion, annotations/log entries, finale tease/requirements\\r\\n- Ship's Log is derived by `src/domain/ships-log.ts`, not stored as duplicate display history\\r\\n- Unseen state is cross-device per player access identity; ceremony playback remains device-local\\r\\n\\r\\n## Development presets\\r\\n\\r\\nRun `npm run db:preset -- <preset>` to reset the development campaign. Supported values:\\r\\n\\r\\n- `awaiting-first-release` (default seed)\\r\\n- `active-chapter`\\r\\n- `mid-voyage`\\r\\n- `artifact-award-ready`\\r\\n- `side-quest-active`\\r\\n- `nearly-complete-shell`\\r\\n- `all-empty`\\r\\n- `long-log`\\r\\n- `mobile-stress-test`\\r\\n\\r\\nPresets are CLI-only, safe to repeat, development-content-only, and not exposed to players or production UI.\\r\\n\\r\\n## Commands\\r\\n\\r\\n- Start everything: `npm run dev:full`\\r\\n- Stop: `npm run dev:stop`\\r\\n- Complete validation: `npm run validate`\\r\\n- SQLite generate/migrate/seed: `npm run db:generate && npm run db:migrate && npm run db:seed`\\r\\n- MySQL companion migration: apply `db:migrate:mysql:init` then `db:migrate:mysql:companion`\\r\\n\\r\\nLocal development defaults come from `.env.example`: player phrase `development-moonwake`; GM `kato` / `development-captain-only`. Never use them outside an isolated local development database.\\r\\n\\r\\n## Validation state\\r\\n\\r\\n- Strict TypeScript: passing\\r\\n- Unit tests: 13 passing across 5 files\\r\\n- ESLint: passing\\r\\n- Production build: passing\\r\\n- SQLite migrations: clean application of init + companion migration\\r\\n- `mid-voyage` fixture integrity: 12 contiguous events, 12 snapshots, 12 audits\\r\\n- In-app browser: all six sections, deep-link history, artifact dialog, sealed finale, 1440/1920 desktop and 390 mobile states inspected with zero horizontal overflow and no console warnings\\r\\n- Chromium/WebKit automated acceptance and clean-clone validation are part of the final task gate; PARALLEL CONTRACT RULES\\r\\n\\r\\nPhase 2 may simultaneously modify:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Player snapshot types\\r\\n* Campaign domain models\\r\\n* Event definitions\\r\\n* Database schema\\r\\n* Navigation structures\\r\\n* Artifact models\\r\\n* Side-quest models\\r\\n* Map models\\r\\n* Finale state\\r\\n* Unseen-content state\\r\\n* Player-facing transformations\\r\\n\\r\\nTherefore, Phase 3 must avoid tightly coupling to unfinished Phase 2 component implementations.\\r\\n\\r\\nUse stable shared contracts.\\r\\n\\r\\nPreferred approach:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Extend shared domain contracts in clearly isolated files.\\r\\n* Keep Game Master UI components separate from Player components.\\r\\n* Keep administrative API schemas explicitly versioned or validated.\\r\\n* Use additive database migrations.\\r\\n* Avoid destructive schema renames.\\r\\n* Avoid broad formatting or folder reorganizations.\\r\\n* Avoid rewriting shared files unnecessarily.\\r\\n* Keep shared changes small and documented.\\r\\n* Add compatibility adapters where practical.\\r\\n* Isolate temporary development controls behind development-only gates.\\r\\n\\r\\nBefore changing any shared contract, document:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Existing behavior\\r\\n2. Required Phase 3 extension\\r\\n3. Why the extension is necessary\\r\\n4. Whether Phase 2 is likely to touch the same contract\\r\\n5. Compatibility strategy\\r\\n6. Migration strategy\\r\\n7. Tests that protect both old and new behavior\\r\\n\\r\\nCreate:\\r\\n\\r\\n`docs/parallel-development-phase-3.md`\\r\\n\\r\\nThis document must identify:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Base commit\\r\\n* Phase 3 branch\\r\\n* Shared files modified\\r\\n* Shared schemas modified\\r\\n* New events introduced\\r\\n* New API contracts introduced\\r\\n* Expected Phase 2 integration points\\r\\n* Likely merge-conflict areas\\r\\n* Recommended integration order\\r\\n* Post-merge validation requirements\\r\\n\\r\\nUpdate it throughout the task.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 3. PRIMARY OBJECTIVE\\r\\n\\r\\nBuild the complete Game Master Command Center required to operate the Forever Treasure Tall Tale.\\r\\n\\r\\nThe Command Center must allow the authenticated Game Master to:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Understand the entire campaignÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s current state immediately\\r\\n2. See exactly what the player currently sees\\r\\n3. Prepare and release chapters\\r\\n4. Release hints progressively\\r\\n5. Control map discoveries and route reveals\\r\\n6. Award and connect artifacts\\r\\n7. Discover and advance side quests\\r\\n8. Add journal annotations and narrative updates\\r\\n9. Trigger player-facing events\\r\\n10. Control Finale readiness without exposing real finale content\\r\\n11. Pause, resume, revert, and recover campaign progression\\r\\n12. Preview changes before releasing them\\r\\n13. Schedule or stage future releases without prematurely exposing content\\r\\n14. See player connection and synchronization state\\r\\n15. Inspect event and audit history\\r\\n16. Operate safely under time pressure\\r\\n17. Recover from mistakes without corrupting campaign history\\r\\n\\r\\nAll actions must be real, persisted, authorized, audited, event-driven, and reflected in the Player Companion through the existing live-update architecture.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 4. SCOPE BOUNDARY\\r\\n\\r\\nThis phase includes:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Game Master dashboard shell\\r\\n* Campaign overview\\r\\n* Live operations workspace\\r\\n* Chapter operations\\r\\n* Hint operations\\r\\n* Voyage Chart operations\\r\\n* Artifact operations\\r\\n* Side-quest operations\\r\\n* Journal and narrative operations\\r\\n* ShipÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s Log event controls\\r\\n* Finale readiness controls\\r\\n* Player-view mirror\\r\\n* Event queue and release staging\\r\\n* Undo and recovery\\r\\n* Campaign pause and resume\\r\\n* Audit history\\r\\n* Diagnostics\\r\\n* Operational keyboard shortcuts\\r\\n* Responsive laptop and tablet layouts\\r\\n* Automated validation\\r\\n* Development-only test controls\\r\\n* GitHub branch publication and pull request\\r\\n\\r\\nThis phase does not include:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Final real Tall Tale story content\\r\\n* Real finale text\\r\\n* Real romantic messages\\r\\n* Private photographs\\r\\n* Production deployment\\r\\n* Replacing the Player Companion implementation\\r\\n* Merging directly into `main`\\r\\n* Building a general-purpose multi-customer CMS\\r\\n* Public user registration\\r\\n* Multiple independent Game Masters unless existing architecture already supports it naturally\\r\\n* Rich visual story authoring comparable to a commercial website builder\\r\\n\\r\\nUse development-only content.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 5. OPERATIONAL DESIGN PRINCIPLES\\r\\n\\r\\nThe Command Center must prioritize:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. **State truth**\\r\\n2. **Accident prevention**\\r\\n3. **Fast operation**\\r\\n4. **Clear consequences**\\r\\n5. **Easy recovery**\\r\\n6. **Player privacy**\\r\\n7. **Atmospheric coherence**\\r\\n\\r\\nEvery displayed status must reflect actual server state.\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not show:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“Player connectedÃ¢â‚¬Â without real connection evidence\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“SavedÃ¢â‚¬Â before persistence completes\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“ReleasedÃ¢â‚¬Â before event creation succeeds\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“SyncedÃ¢â‚¬Â while the player is behind\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“Undo availableÃ¢â‚¬Â when reversal is impossible\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“PreviewÃ¢â‚¬Â that secretly mutates production state\\r\\n\\r\\nThe interface must distinguish clearly between:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Draft\\r\\n* Prepared\\r\\n* Scheduled\\r\\n* Queued\\r\\n* Releasing\\r\\n* Released\\r\\n* Viewed\\r\\n* Solved\\r\\n* Completed\\r\\n* Reverted\\r\\n* Failed\\r\\n\\r\\nNo optimistic fiction. Interfaces already lie enough without joining a pirate crew.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 6. COMMAND CENTER INFORMATION ARCHITECTURE\\r\\n\\r\\nCreate a cohesive Game Master information architecture.\\r\\n\\r\\nRecommended major workspaces:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Command Deck\\r\\n* Chapters\\r\\n* Hints\\r\\n* Voyage\\r\\n* Artifacts\\r\\n* Side Quests\\r\\n* Journal\\r\\n* Events\\r\\n* Player View\\r\\n* Recovery\\r\\n* Diagnostics\\r\\n\\r\\nPossible logical routes:\\r\\n\\r\\n* `/quartermaster`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/chapters`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/voyage`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/artifacts`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/quests`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/journal`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/events`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/player-view`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/recovery`\\r\\n* `/quartermaster/diagnostics`\\r\\n\\r\\nUse the existing authenticated layout.\\r\\n\\r\\nRequirements:\\r\\n\\r\\n* URL-reflected navigation\\r\\n* Browser back and forward support\\r\\n* Deep-link safety\\r\\n* Authentication enforcement\\r\\n* Keyboard accessibility\\r\\n* Responsive behavior\\r\\n* Stable live state while navigating\\r\\n* No full application reload for ordinary navigation\\r\\n* No exposure of admin routes through player navigation\\r\\n\\r\\nDocument the final route structure.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 7. VISUAL DIRECTION\\r\\n\\r\\nThe Game Master interface should share the same world as the Player Companion while using a more operational visual language.\\r\\n\\r\\nSuggested metaphor:\\r\\n\\r\\n* CaptainÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s chart table\\r\\n* QuartermasterÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s ledger\\r\\n* Brass control instruments\\r\\n* Signal lanterns\\r\\n* Sealed dispatches\\r\\n* Navigational plotting tools\\r\\n* Ship-status instrumentation\\r\\n\\r\\nUse:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Deep charcoal and ocean-dark foundations\\r\\n* Weathered surfaces\\r\\n* Brass controls\\r\\n* Parchment content areas\\r\\n* Muted crimson for warnings\\r\\n* Moonlit cyan-green for live synchronization\\r\\n* Amber for staged or pending actions\\r\\n* Red only for actual danger, destructive actions, or failed synchronization\\r\\n\\r\\nAvoid:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Generic admin-template styling\\r\\n* Bright neon control panels\\r\\n* Excess glassmorphism\\r\\n* Card-grid monotony\\r\\n* Decorative cursive for operational text\\r\\n* Low contrast\\r\\n* Tiny labels\\r\\n* Hidden critical status\\r\\n* Excess animation during routine operation\\r\\n\\r\\nThe dashboard should be beautiful, but clarity outranks theatrical flourish.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 8. RESPONSIVE TARGETS\\r\\n\\r\\nPrimary use:\\r\\n\\r\\n* 2560Ãƒâ€”1440 secondary monitor\\r\\n* 1920Ãƒâ€”1080 desktop\\r\\n* 1440Ãƒâ€”900 laptop\\r\\n* Split-screen desktop\\r\\n* Tablet-sized emergency operation\\r\\n\\r\\nSupport at minimum:\\r\\n\\r\\n* 2560Ãƒâ€”1440\\r\\n* 1920Ãƒâ€”1080\\r\\n* 1440Ãƒâ€”900\\r\\n* 1280Ãƒâ€”800\\r\\n* 1024Ãƒâ€”768\\r\\n* 834Ãƒâ€”1194 tablet portrait\\r\\n* 1194Ãƒâ€”834 tablet landscape\\r\\n\\r\\nThe Game Master interface does not need to be optimized as a full iPhone authoring system, but it should remain usable for emergency actions on a phone.\\r\\n\\r\\nAt narrow widths, prioritize:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Current state\\r\\n* Pause\\r\\n* Player View\\r\\n* Current chapter\\r\\n* Release hint\\r\\n* Emergency recovery\\r\\n* Undo last action\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not expose every control simultaneously on small screens.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 9. COMMAND DECK OVERVIEW\\r\\n\\r\\nCreate a Command Deck landing page that answers, within seconds:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Is the campaign running?\\r\\n* Is the player connected?\\r\\n* Is the player synchronized?\\r\\n* What chapter is active?\\r\\n* What is the current objective?\\r\\n* What has been prepared next?\\r\\n* Are any releases queued?\\r\\n* Are any side quests active?\\r\\n* What artifacts have been collected?\\r\\n* Are there warnings?\\r\\n* What was the last action?\\r\\n* Can that action be undone?\\r\\n* What does the player currently see?\\r\\n\\r\\nThe page should include:\\r\\n\\r\\n## Campaign Status\\r\\n\\r\\n* Campaign name\\r\\n* Campaign state\\r\\n* Started time\\r\\n* Paused status\\r\\n* Active chapter\\r\\n* Current progression sequence\\r\\n* Last persisted update\\r\\n* Latest player synchronization sequence\\r\\n\\r\\n## Player Connection\\r\\n\\r\\n* Connected\\r\\n* Disconnected\\r\\n* Reconnecting\\r\\n* Last seen\\r\\n* Last acknowledged event or snapshot sequence\\r\\n* Current player route\\r\\n* Device count if reliably supported\\r\\n* Potentially stale session warning\\r\\n\\r\\n## Immediate Operations\\r\\n\\r\\n* Prepare next chapter\\r\\n* Release prepared chapter\\r\\n* Release next hint\\r\\n* Mark current objective solved\\r\\n* Pause campaign\\r\\n* Open Player View\\r\\n* Undo last reversible action\\r\\n\\r\\n## Upcoming\\r\\n\\r\\n* Prepared chapters\\r\\n* Prepared hints\\r\\n* Staged map reveals\\r\\n* Staged artifact awards\\r\\n* Staged side-quest updates\\r\\n* Scheduled actions if scheduling is implemented\\r\\n\\r\\n## Recent Events\\r\\n\\r\\n* Latest player-facing progression\\r\\n* Latest Game Master actions\\r\\n* Failed actions\\r\\n* Reversals\\r\\n* Synchronization warnings\\r\\n\\r\\nAvoid vanity metrics.\\r\\n\\r\\nThe Game Master does not need an analytics graph proving that one player viewed one clue.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 10. LIVE PLAYER CONNECTION STATUS\\r\\n\\r\\nImplement truthful player connection monitoring.\\r\\n\\r\\nUse real evidence from:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Active SSE connections\\r\\n* Heartbeats\\r\\n* Last event acknowledgment if supported\\r\\n* Snapshot fetches\\r\\n* Client presence records with expiration\\r\\n* Visibility or activity information only where responsibly available\\r\\n\\r\\nClearly distinguish:\\r\\n\\r\\n* No active connection\\r\\n* Connection active\\r\\n* Connection recently lost\\r\\n* Player reconnecting\\r\\n* Player state potentially stale\\r\\n* Multiple player devices connected\\r\\n* Unknown state\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not claim the player is actively reading simply because a browser connection exists.\\r\\n\\r\\nPossible labels:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“Connected to journalÃ¢â‚¬Â\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“Last synchronized 8 seconds agoÃ¢â‚¬Â\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“No active journal connectionÃ¢â‚¬Â\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“Two player devices connectedÃ¢â‚¬Â\\r\\n* Ã¢â‚¬Å“Player may be viewing stale stateÃ¢â‚¬Â\\r\\n\\r\\nCreate tests for expiration and stale-presence behavior.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 11. PLAYER-VIEW MIRROR\\r\\n\\r\\nBuild a secure Player View workspace.\\r\\n\\r\\nThis must allow the Game Master to inspect what the player is permitted to see.\\r\\n\\r\\nPreferred implementation:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Reuse player visibility-filtering logic\\r\\n* Generate the same sanitized player snapshot\\r\\n* Render through a preview adapter or embedded isolated preview\\r\\n* Prevent Game Master-only metadata from appearing in the mirrored content\\r\\n\\r\\nModes:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Current Player View\\r\\n* Desktop preview\\r\\n* Mobile preview\\r\\n* Reduced-motion preview\\r\\n* Muted preview\\r\\n* Future change preview\\r\\n* Reveal ceremony preview\\r\\n\\r\\nCritical distinction:\\r\\n\\r\\n## Current Player View\\r\\n\\r\\nRepresents actual released player state.\\r\\n\\r\\n## Preview\\r\\n\\r\\nRepresents a hypothetical proposed action without persistence or event publication.\\r\\n\\r\\nPreview must never:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Change campaign state\\r\\n* Increment event sequence\\r\\n* Mark content viewed\\r\\n* Award an artifact\\r\\n* Release a hint\\r\\n* Trigger real player audio\\r\\n* Send an SSE event\\r\\n\\r\\nClearly label previews.\\r\\n\\r\\nAdd a visual border or watermark such as:\\r\\n\\r\\nÃ¢â‚¬Å“PREVIEW Ã¢â‚¬â€ NOT RELEASEDÃ¢â‚¬Â\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not rely only on color to distinguish preview.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 12. PROPOSED-ACTION PREVIEW SYSTEM\\r\\n\\r\\nBefore important releases, allow the Game Master to preview the exact resulting player state.\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Chapter release\\r\\n* Hint release\\r\\n* Map location reveal\\r\\n* Route reveal\\r\\n* Artifact award\\r\\n* Artifact connection\\r\\n* Side-quest discovery\\r\\n* Finale tease\\r\\n* Journal annotation\\r\\n\\r\\nThe preview system should:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Validate proposed action\\r\\n2. Compute projected campaign snapshot\\r\\n3. Apply player visibility filtering\\r\\n4. Render projected player state\\r\\n5. Show resulting log entry\\r\\n6. Show resulting event type\\r\\n7. Show affected related systems\\r\\n8. Show whether undo will be available\\r\\n9. Show any unmet prerequisites\\r\\n10. Make no persistent changes\\r\\n\\r\\nUse server-side projection where practical to prevent client divergence.\\r\\n\\r\\nTest that preview does not mutate state.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 13. ACTION SAFETY LEVELS\\r\\n\\r\\nClassify operations by risk.\\r\\n\\r\\n## Low Risk\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Open preview\\r\\n* Refresh status\\r\\n* View audit entry\\r\\n* Navigate\\r\\n* Replay local preview\\r\\n\\r\\nMay execute immediately.\\r\\n\\r\\n## Medium Risk\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Add draft annotation\\r\\n* Prepare chapter\\r\\n* Prepare hint\\r\\n* Stage map reveal\\r\\n* Stage artifact award\\r\\n\\r\\nRequire clear acknowledgment but not typed confirmation.\\r\\n\\r\\n## High Risk\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Release chapter\\r\\n* Award artifact\\r\\n* Complete side quest\\r\\n* Reveal route\\r\\n* Tease Finale\\r\\n* Pause campaign\\r\\n* Resume campaign where queued actions exist\\r\\n* Undo progression\\r\\n\\r\\nRequire a strong confirmation dialog showing:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Exact action\\r\\n* Player-visible consequence\\r\\n* Related chapter or item\\r\\n* Current state\\r\\n* Resulting state\\r\\n* Whether theatrical animation will trigger\\r\\n* Whether undo is available\\r\\n\\r\\n## Critical Risk\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Force progression\\r\\n* Reset development campaign\\r\\n* Revert multiple events\\r\\n* Skip required chapter prerequisites\\r\\n* Unlock Finale\\r\\n* Factory-reset campaign state\\r\\n\\r\\nRequire:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Typed confirmation phrase\\r\\n* Explicit scope\\r\\n* Reason field\\r\\n* Confirmation that player impact is understood\\r\\n\\r\\nFinale unlock must remain disabled or development-only during this phase.\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not make every action require typing. That would m…18 tokens truncated…e 2 integration impact.\\r\\n\\r\\nKeep player-facing payloads minimal and sanitized.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 53. CONTRACT VERSIONING\\r\\n\\r\\nWhere shared contracts are likely to evolve during Phase 2:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Use schema validation\\r\\n* Add optional fields where safe\\r\\n* Avoid breaking required-field changes\\r\\n* Consider explicit schema versions for event payloads\\r\\n* Provide compatibility handling\\r\\n* Reject unknown unsafe versions\\r\\n* Test older snapshot compatibility where relevant\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not build an elaborate public API versioning program.\\r\\n\\r\\nThe goal is safe parallel integration, not bureaucratic pageantry.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 54. GAME MASTER SEED DATA\\r\\n\\r\\nExtend development seed data to exercise the Command Center.\\r\\n\\r\\nInclude:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Multiple chapter states\\r\\n* Multiple hints\\r\\n* Multiple map states\\r\\n* Multiple artifact states\\r\\n* Multiple side-quest states\\r\\n* Staged action examples\\r\\n* Reversible event examples\\r\\n* Player connection examples where simulation is supported\\r\\n* Audit records\\r\\n* Failed command sample in test fixtures\\r\\n* Finale teaser placeholder\\r\\n* Paused campaign preset\\r\\n* Stale player preset\\r\\n* Long event history preset\\r\\n\\r\\nUse generic development-only content.\\r\\n\\r\\nNo real surprise content.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 55. AUTOMATED TESTING\\r\\n\\r\\n## Unit tests\\r\\n\\r\\nTest:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Command authorization\\r\\n* Command validation\\r\\n* Legal chapter transitions\\r\\n* Illegal chapter transitions\\r\\n* Hint release ordering\\r\\n* Map reveal prerequisites\\r\\n* Artifact award prerequisites\\r\\n* Side-quest transitions\\r\\n* Pause restrictions\\r\\n* Resume revalidation\\r\\n* Preview nonmutation\\r\\n* Idempotency\\r\\n* Reversal dependency analysis\\r\\n* Presence expiration\\r\\n* Delivery-state calculation\\r\\n* Player-snapshot sanitization\\r\\n* Audit transformation\\r\\n* Permission capabilities\\r\\n\\r\\n## Integration tests\\r\\n\\r\\nTest:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Command transaction atomicity\\r\\n* Event and audit created together\\r\\n* Snapshot update\\r\\n* Event publication\\r\\n* Duplicate command handling\\r\\n* Stale version conflict\\r\\n* Pause blocking\\r\\n* Prepared action becoming stale\\r\\n* Undo creating reversal event\\r\\n* Multi-action dependency protection\\r\\n* Player presence updates\\r\\n* Reconciliation request\\r\\n* Admin/player authorization boundary\\r\\n* Schema migration integrity\\r\\n\\r\\n## End-to-end tests\\r\\n\\r\\nTest:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Game Master logs in.\\r\\n2. Command Deck loads truthful state.\\r\\n3. Player View matches sanitized player state.\\r\\n4. Preview does not mutate state.\\r\\n5. Chapter prepares.\\r\\n6. Chapter releases.\\r\\n7. Active player receives event.\\r\\n8. Hint releases.\\r\\n9. Map location reveals.\\r\\n10. Artifact awards.\\r\\n11. Side quest discovers.\\r\\n12. Side quest advances.\\r\\n13. Narrative annotation releases.\\r\\n14. Campaign pauses.\\r\\n15. Progression is rejected while paused.\\r\\n16. Campaign resumes.\\r\\n17. Staged action is revalidated.\\r\\n18. Undo creates reversal.\\r\\n19. Audit log records actions.\\r\\n20. Duplicate release is rejected.\\r\\n21. Stale tab conflict is handled.\\r\\n22. Player disconnect is displayed.\\r\\n23. Reconciliation succeeds.\\r\\n24. Emergency control is confirmed and audited.\\r\\n25. Development reset remains production-gated.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 56. LIVE TWO-BROWSER VALIDATION\\r\\n\\r\\nUse separate Playwright browser contexts:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Player context\\r\\n* Game Master context\\r\\n\\r\\nValidate:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Player authentication boundaries\\r\\n* Game Master session boundaries\\r\\n* Live event delivery\\r\\n* Player View accuracy\\r\\n* Connection status\\r\\n* Delivery versus synchronization distinction\\r\\n* Background tab behavior\\r\\n* Multiple player devices where supported\\r\\n* Disconnect and reconnect\\r\\n* Stale player detection\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not fake live delivery with shared client memory.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 57. VISUAL VALIDATION\\r\\n\\r\\nCapture and inspect:\\r\\n\\r\\n## Command Deck\\r\\n\\r\\n* 2560Ãƒâ€”1440\\r\\n* 1920Ãƒâ€”1080\\r\\n* 1440Ãƒâ€”900\\r\\n* 1280Ãƒâ€”800\\r\\n\\r\\n## Tablet and narrow\\r\\n\\r\\n* 1194Ãƒâ€”834\\r\\n* 834Ãƒâ€”1194\\r\\n* 430Ãƒâ€”932 emergency view\\r\\n\\r\\n## Workspaces\\r\\n\\r\\nCapture:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Chapter list\\r\\n* Chapter release confirmation\\r\\n* Hint quick control\\r\\n* Voyage operations\\r\\n* Artifact award preview\\r\\n* Side-quest control\\r\\n* Event staging\\r\\n* Player View desktop\\r\\n* Player View mobile\\r\\n* Recovery dependency warning\\r\\n* Emergency operations\\r\\n* Audit history\\r\\n* Diagnostics\\r\\n* Command palette\\r\\n\\r\\nInspect:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Hierarchy\\r\\n* Status truth\\r\\n* Control density\\r\\n* Accidental-click risk\\r\\n* Truncated text\\r\\n* Confirmation clarity\\r\\n* Responsive collapse\\r\\n* Button collisions\\r\\n* Focus state\\r\\n* Warning differentiation\\r\\n* Player View sizing\\r\\n* Table overflow\\r\\n* Long title behavior\\r\\n* Long audit history\\r\\n* Small-screen emergency usability\\r\\n\\r\\nPerform at least two deliberate visual refinement passes.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 58. USABILITY STRESS TEST\\r\\n\\r\\nSimulate operating the dashboard during active gameplay.\\r\\n\\r\\nMeasure or observe:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Time to open active chapter\\r\\n* Time to release next hint\\r\\n* Time to open Player View\\r\\n* Time to pause campaign\\r\\n* Time to find last action\\r\\n* Time to undo last reversible action\\r\\n* Number of interactions required\\r\\n* Whether critical status remains visible\\r\\n* Whether confirmation text is understandable at a glance\\r\\n\\r\\nTarget:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Common read operations in one interaction\\r\\n* Common safe operations in one to two interactions\\r\\n* High-risk release in a short but deliberate confirmation sequence\\r\\n* Emergency pause reachable quickly but not accidentally\\r\\n\\r\\nDocument usability findings.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 59. ACCESSIBILITY\\r\\n\\r\\nValidate:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Keyboard navigation\\r\\n* Focus order\\r\\n* Visible focus\\r\\n* Semantic landmarks\\r\\n* Accessible tables\\r\\n* Accessible dialogs\\r\\n* Dialog focus trapping\\r\\n* Escape behavior\\r\\n* Screen-reader status updates\\r\\n* Non-color status indicators\\r\\n* Sufficient contrast\\r\\n* Text scaling\\r\\n* Reduced motion\\r\\n* Touch targets\\r\\n* Command palette accessibility\\r\\n* Preview labeling\\r\\n* Error announcement\\r\\n* Audit-log readability\\r\\n\\r\\nNo essential administrative function may require hover.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 60. PERFORMANCE\\r\\n\\r\\nMeasure:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Initial Command Deck load\\r\\n* Route navigation\\r\\n* Large event history\\r\\n* Player View rendering\\r\\n* Preview generation\\r\\n* Search\\r\\n* Command palette\\r\\n* SSE monitoring\\r\\n* Long-running session\\r\\n* Multiple player connections\\r\\n* Database query count\\r\\n\\r\\nAvoid:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Polling every panel independently\\r\\n* Refetch storms\\r\\n* Rendering thousands of audit rows\\r\\n* Shipping player-only heavy assets unnecessarily\\r\\n* Shipping admin code to the player bundle\\r\\n* Continuous decorative animation\\r\\n* Giant client-side campaign objects when a filtered view will do\\r\\n\\r\\nUse pagination or virtualization where needed.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 61. SECURITY VALIDATION\\r\\n\\r\\nVerify:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Admin routes require authentication\\r\\n* Admin APIs require authorization\\r\\n* Player token cannot call admin commands\\r\\n* CSRF protection works\\r\\n* Rate limiting works\\r\\n* Session cookies are secure\\r\\n* Preview does not leak secrets\\r\\n* Player View uses sanitized data\\r\\n* Draft content does not leak\\r\\n* Audit data does not leak\\r\\n* Hidden map data does not leak\\r\\n* Hidden artifact data does not leak\\r\\n* Finale content does not leak\\r\\n* Development controls are production-gated\\r\\n* No secrets are committed\\r\\n* No arbitrary HTML or script injection\\r\\n* No IDOR between campaigns\\r\\n* No campaign enumeration\\r\\n* No mass-assignment state mutation\\r\\n\\r\\nDocument security results.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 62. STARTUP AND VALIDATION EXPERIENCE\\r\\n\\r\\nPreserve the one-command startup system.\\r\\n\\r\\nPhase 3 must not require the user to manually start a second pile of services.\\r\\n\\r\\nIntegrate any new migration or setup requirements into the existing launcher.\\r\\n\\r\\nPreserve or improve:\\r\\n\\r\\n* One-command startup\\r\\n* One-command shutdown\\r\\n* One-command validation\\r\\n* Exact URLs printed\\r\\n* Development login guidance\\r\\n* Database health handling\\r\\n* Idempotent migration\\r\\n* Seed verification\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not return the project to a fifteen-command summoning ritual.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 63. CLEAN-CLONE VALIDATION\\r\\n\\r\\nFrom a fresh clone of the Phase 3 branch:\\r\\n\\r\\n```bash\\r\\ngit clone --branch feature/game-master-command-center https://github.com/Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT.git\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nUsing only committed files:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Install dependencies.\\r\\n2. Create local environment.\\r\\n3. Start database.\\r\\n4. Apply migrations.\\r\\n5. Seed development content.\\r\\n6. Start application.\\r\\n7. Open Game Master dashboard.\\r\\n8. Open Player Companion.\\r\\n9. Run validation.\\r\\n10. Run production build.\\r\\n11. Execute key two-browser workflows.\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not copy local machine state.\\r\\n\\r\\nFix failures and repeat.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 64. PHASE 2 INTEGRATION PREPARATION\\r\\n\\r\\nBecause Phase 2 is running in parallel, prepare for later reconciliation.\\r\\n\\r\\nAt the end:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Fetch latest `origin/main`.\\r\\n2. Fetch any known Phase 2 branch.\\r\\n3. Do not merge Phase 2 automatically unless explicitly instructed.\\r\\n4. Compare changed-file overlap.\\r\\n5. Identify likely conflicts.\\r\\n6. Document integration order.\\r\\n7. Document shared contract reconciliation.\\r\\n8. Document migrations order.\\r\\n9. Document tests required after merge.\\r\\n\\r\\nIf Phase 2 has already merged into `main` before Phase 3 completion, rebase Phase 3 onto the updated `origin/main` only if it can be done safely.\\r\\n\\r\\nAfter rebase:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Resolve conflicts deliberately\\r\\n* Preserve Phase 2 player behavior\\r\\n* Re-run the full test suite\\r\\n* Re-run visual validation\\r\\n* Re-run clean-clone validation\\r\\n* Never force-push unless the branch is private to this single session and safe force-with-lease has been explicitly authorized\\r\\n\\r\\nDefault: do not rewrite remote branch history.\\r\\n\\r\\nPrefer merge from `origin/main` into the Phase 3 branch if the branch has already been shared.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 65. DOCUMENTATION\\r\\n\\r\\nUpdate relevant documentation:\\r\\n\\r\\n* `README.md`\\r\\n* `docs/architecture.md`\\r\\n* `docs/story-state-machine.md`\\r\\n* `docs/game-master-controls.md`\\r\\n* `docs/security.md`\\r\\n* `docs/testing.md`\\r\\n* `docs/known-limitations.md`\\r\\n* `docs/codex-handoff.md`\\r\\n* `docs/parallel-development-phase-3.md`\\r\\n\\r\\nCreate:\\r\\n\\r\\n* `docs/command-center-information-architecture.md`\\r\\n* `docs/admin-command-pipeline.md`\\r\\n* `docs/player-presence-and-sync.md`\\r\\n* `docs/action-risk-model.md`\\r\\n* `docs/recovery-and-reversal.md`\\r\\n* `docs/audit-and-event-history.md`\\r\\n* `docs/phase-2-phase-3-integration.md`\\r\\n\\r\\nUse diagrams for:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Command execution pipeline\\r\\n* Preview flow\\r\\n* Release flow\\r\\n* Player delivery and synchronization\\r\\n* Reversal dependency flow\\r\\n* Pause and resume\\r\\n* Staged action lifecycle\\r\\n* Branch integration plan\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 66. CODEX HANDOFF\\r\\n\\r\\nUpdate:\\r\\n\\r\\n`docs/codex-handoff.md`\\r\\n\\r\\nInclude:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Phase 3 branch\\r\\n* Base commit\\r\\n* Current status\\r\\n* Command Center routes\\r\\n* Authentication behavior\\r\\n* Major workspaces\\r\\n* New domain commands\\r\\n* New events\\r\\n* New migrations\\r\\n* Development controls\\r\\n* Startup command\\r\\n* Validation command\\r\\n* Known limitations\\r\\n* Shared-file overlap with Phase 2\\r\\n* Required integration steps\\r\\n* Latest validated Phase 3 commit\\r\\n* Pull request reference\\r\\n* Recommended next task\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not claim Phase 3 is integrated into `main` until it actually is.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 67. COMMIT STRATEGY\\r\\n\\r\\nUse logical commits.\\r\\n\\r\\nSuggested sequence:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Add Command Center shell and navigation\\r\\n2. Add administrative command pipeline\\r\\n3. Add chapter and hint operations\\r\\n4. Add voyage and artifact operations\\r\\n5. Add side-quest and narrative operations\\r\\n6. Add player presence and Player View\\r\\n7. Add staging, recovery, and audit systems\\r\\n8. Add tests and visual refinements\\r\\n9. Add documentation and integration plan\\r\\n\\r\\nActual commit boundaries should follow coherent implementation.\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not use meaningless commit messages.\\r\\n\\r\\nBefore every commit:\\r\\n\\r\\n```bash\\r\\ngit status --short\\r\\ngit diff --check\\r\\ngit diff\\r\\ngit diff --staged\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nReview for secrets and unrelated files.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 68. PUSH AND PULL REQUEST\\r\\n\\r\\nPush only to:\\r\\n\\r\\n`origin/feature/game-master-command-center`\\r\\n\\r\\nCommand:\\r\\n\\r\\n```bash\\r\\ngit push -u origin feature/game-master-command-center\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not push Phase 3 commits directly to `main`.\\r\\n\\r\\nAfter pushing:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Fetch origin.\\r\\n2. Verify local branch SHA matches remote branch SHA.\\r\\n3. Confirm working tree is clean.\\r\\n4. Create or update a pull request:\\r\\n\\r\\nBase:\\r\\n\\r\\n`main`\\r\\n\\r\\nHead:\\r\\n\\r\\n`feature/game-master-command-center`\\r\\n\\r\\nSuggested title:\\r\\n\\r\\n`Build expanded Game Master Command Center`\\r\\n\\r\\nThe pull request body must include:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Summary\\r\\n* Screenshots\\r\\n* Architecture changes\\r\\n* Migrations\\r\\n* New events\\r\\n* New commands\\r\\n* Security notes\\r\\n* Test totals\\r\\n* Clean-clone result\\r\\n* Shared-file overlap with Phase 2\\r\\n* Known integration conflicts\\r\\n* Required merge order\\r\\n* Post-merge validation checklist\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not merge the pull request.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 69. staged actions, recent events, immediate commands |\r\n| Chapters | Legal state transitions, prerequisites, prepare/release/solve/complete previews |\r\n| Hints | Ordered hints, preparation, quick next-hint release |\r\n| Voyage | Player-safe map plotting and reveal controls |\r\n| Artifacts | Award state, preview, uniqueness protection |\r\n| Side Quests | Discovery and validated advancement |\r\n| Journal | Plain-text narrative composition and release |\r\n| Event Staging | Persisted prepared actions, stale-state labeling, ordered history |\r\n| Player View | Sanitized released-state mirror with desktop/mobile modes |\r\n| Recovery | Latest-safe reversal, historic save points, emergency pause |\r\n| Audit | Actor, action, outcome, correlation, failures |\r\n| Diagnostics | Database, SSE, sequence lag, capabilities, reconciliation |\r\n| Emergency Operations | Responsive Pause/Resume, Player View, Next Hint, and Undo dock |\r\n\r\nAll release dialogs show projected sequence, event, affected systems, ceremony behavior, undo availability, and unmet prerequisites.\r\n\r\n## Domain architecture\r\n\r\nAdded:\r\n\r\n- `PreparedAction`\r\n- `CommandExecution`\r\n- Project filtering should use the strongest available evidence, in this approximate order:\r\n\r\n1. Explicit project ID or repository association.\r\n2. Exact project name.\r\n3.
