[{"text": "Script completed\nWall time 0.3 seconds\nOutput:\n", "type": "input_text"}, {"text": "Exit code: 0\nWall time: 0.2 seconds\nOutput:\n\n===== README.md =====\n\r\n# The Forever Treasure Companion\n\nProduction-oriented foundation and fully automated local demo for a private nautical-fantasy date-night Tall Tale. All committed story material is generic development seed content; no final surprise content belongs in this public repository.\n\n## Current phase\n\nPhase two expands the production foundation into the complete Player Companion shell: multi-state Journal, Voyage Chart, Treasure Altar, Side-Quest Ledger, event-derived Shipâ€™s Log, sealed Finale, persistent objective, companion navigation, cross-device unseen state, preferences, and fullscreen-friendly operation. All committed content remains fictional development seed material.\n\n## Stack\n\n- Next.js 16 App Router, React 19, strict TypeScript\n- Prisma 6.19 with SQLite for local/test and an equivalent MySQL production schema\n- Framer Motion with pinned compatible motion packages\n- bcrypt password/access-code hashing, server-side database sessions, CSRF tokens, and rate limiting\n- Vitest and Playwright\n\n## Reproducible setup\n\nRequirements: Windows PowerShell 5.1+, Node.js 24, npm 11, and Git. MySQL 8 is only required for production parity; the supported local/demo/test strategy is SQLite.\n\n### Fastest Windows demo\n\nAfter cloning, double-click `Start Forever Treasure Dev.cmd`, or run:\n\n```powershell\nnpm run dev:full\n```\n\nThe launcher creates an ignored `.env` when absent, installs the exact lockfile, generates Prisma, applies migrations, seeds generic disposable data, starts the app, checks health, and prints the URLs and credentials. Network/UNC workspaces are mirrored to an ignored local runtime under `%LOCALAPPDATA%` because Node and SQLite are unreliable on network shares. Stop it with `npm run dev:stop`.\n\n- Player: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\n- Player phrase: `development-moonwake`\n- GM: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\n- GM development[REDACTED: LOGIN CREDENTIAL]\n\nThese values are disposable local defaults, never production credentials. To opt into LAN access, run `powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File scripts/start-dev.ps1 -Lan`; Windows Firewall and network policy still determine whether another device can connect.\n\n### Portable manual setup\n\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT.git\ncd treasurehuntSoT\nnpm ci\ncp .env.example .env\nnpm run db:generate\nnpm run db:migrate\nnpm run db:seed\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nSet unique local values in `.env`; the seedâ€™s fallback values are development-only and must never be used outside an isolated development database. Player path: `/tale/development-forever-treasure`. GM path: `/quartermaster`.\n\n## Commands\n\n| Command                           | Purpose                                                                |\n| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `npm run dev:full`                | Idempotent setup, migration, seed, health check, and background server |\n| `npm run dev:stop`                | Safely stop only the recorded development process                      |\n| `npm run validate`                | Fresh DB, format/lint/types/unit/E2E/a11y/build/restart proof          |\n| `npm run dev`                     | Next.js only; assumes setup is already complete                        |\n| `npm run build`                   | Production build                                                       |\n| `npm start`                       | Start production build                                                 |\n| `npm test`                        | Six deterministic unit tests                                           |\n| `npm run test:e2e`                | Five Chromium/WebKit checks plus one Chromium mutation workflow        |\n| `npm run db:migrate`              | Apply local SQLite migrations                                          |\n| `npm run db:seed`                 | Load replaceable development content                                   |\n| `npm run db:preset -- mid-voyage` | Reset to a repeatable development-only companion preset                |\n| `npm run db:generate:mysql`       | Generate production MySQL client                                       |\n\nFull setup, validation stages, output locations, clean-clone instructions, and troubleshooting are in [local development](docs/local-development.md) and [testing](docs/testing.md).\n\n## Repository workflow\n\n`main` is canonical. At task start: fetch, inspect status/remotes, and pull with rebase when clean. At task end: validate, review the complete diff, commit intentionally, fetch/rebase if needed, push without force, and verify local `HEAD` equals `origin/main`. See [Codex handoff](docs/codex-handoff.md).\n\n## Important limitations\n\nThe full story, final art/audio, horizontal scale-out for SSE, and production deployment are later work. The current sound is restrained and procedural; no external copyrighted assets are present. The repository is public, so only generic development content is permitted. See [known limitations](docs/known-limitations.md).\n\n## Next steps\n\nAdd CI for the complete validation gate, exercise the parallel MySQL schema in an integration environment, and replace process-local SSE fan-out before multi-instance deployment. Real surprise content remains blocked until repository visibility is verified private.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\architecture.md =====\n\r\n# Architecture\n\n## Phase 2 companion projection\n\nThe player reads one server-filtered `PublicSnapshot` containing chapters, released hints/annotations, visible map locations/routes, safe artifact states, visible optional mysteries, event-derived log entries, generic finale state, and per-section unseen counts. One SSE connection transports ordered sanitized events; section navigation is client-side and starts no independent polling.\n\nNew normalized records are `MapRoute` and `ViewedContent`. Existing content models gained release, relationship, placement, and safe-label fields rather than duplicating derived display tables. `AudioPreference` now carries the cross-device preference projection; immediate UI choices may also be cached locally for offline startup.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  P[Player journal] -->|snapshot + access cookie| N[Next.js server]\n  G[Quartermaster] -->|session + CSRF| N\n  N -->|Prisma transaction| D[(SQLite dev / MySQL prod)]\n  N -->|ordered SSE + replay| P\n  N --> A[Audit and save-state log]\n```\n\nServer Components enforce initial access. Route handlers own authentication, validation, snapshots, and SSE. `src/server/progression.ts` is the transaction boundary; `src/domain/story.ts` owns state rules. The UI receives only a public projection, never database rows. In-memory publish accelerates same-process delivery while database replay by sequence remains authoritative after reconnect/restart.\n\nMajor dependencies are deliberately limited: Prisma for normalized persistence and transactions, bcryptjs for portable hashes, Zod for payload validation, Framer Motion for accessible choreography, Pino for structured redacted logs, Vitest/Playwright for validation.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\artifact-system.md =====\n\r\n# Artifact system\n\nArtifacts progress through `UNKNOWN`, `SILHOUETTE`, `DISCOVERED`, `AWARDED`, `INSPECTED`, `CONNECTED`, and the future-only `ASSEMBLED` state. Unknown records expose only neutral placement structure. Silhouettes expose only an approved safe name/label. Full names, descriptions, discovery text, related chapter, and award time require a released state.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  U[UNKNOWN] --> S[SILHOUETTE]\n  S --> D[DISCOVERED]\n  D --> A[AWARDED]\n  A --> I[INSPECTED]\n  I --> C[CONNECTED]\n  C -. future phase .-> X[ASSEMBLED]\n```\n\nAssembly groups and neutral positions exist for layout testing, but the seed deliberately does not resemble or name a real finale object.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\asset-licenses.md =====\n\r\n# Asset licenses\n\n## Phase 2\n\nNo third-party story map, game art, photographs, or ripped game audio were added. The companion shell uses CSS, text symbols, and existing procedural audio only. Any later image or sound asset must record source, author, license, modification, and in-product use here before commit.\n\nNo external images, fonts, music, icons, screenshots, or Sea of Thieves assets are included. Parchment, grain, chart markings, constellation, seal, compass needle, and ornamentation are original CSS geometry. The low seal tone is generated at runtime with the Web Audio API and has no source asset. Third-party code licenses remain in package metadata and the lockfile.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\cinematic-system.md =====\n\r\n# Cinematic transition system\n\n`useCinematicTransition` is the shared cancellable sequence runner for arrival, authentication, and progression presentation. A plan has named opening, success, and failure stages. For server-backed commands the real request starts immediately alongside the opening stage. The success branch cannot begin until that request succeeds; a rejected request runs the plan's physical reversal and never reveals authoritative state. Abort on unmount prevents orphaned stage timers, duplicate starts are rejected, and CSS ambience pauses while the document is hidden.\n\nMajor full-motion durations are approximately: first arrival 7.95s, same-session re-entry 1.55s, sign-in success 4.72s, prepare 2.5s, release 4.4s in the Quartermaster plus the 7.75s player reveal, solve 2.55s, relic award 3.6s, map reveal 4.15s, pause 2.55s, resume 2.55s, and undo 2.7s. Gentle motion keeps the order with 280-420ms stages. Reduced motion keeps the narrative ordering with stages capped at 120ms.\n\nThe browser session key `forever-intro:<campaign>` selects the long first arrival once per session. The always-available **Replay introduction** control forces it locally and never changes story state. Development builds also expose an **Animation lab** containing every named sequence; production builds omit it.\n\nAll added material effects are original procedural CSS. No third-party visual or audio assets were added.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\codex-handoff.md =====\n\r\n# Codex handoff\n\n- Canonical repository: `Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT`, branch `main`\n- Phase completed: Phase 2 â€” complete Player Companion shell\n- Repository visibility: **public** as verified 2026-07-16; only fictional development content is permitted\n- Latest validated implementation commit: `0f510a3f218a464259f19efa4f6b8991a8546f4f`\n- Updated: 2026-07-16\n\n## Player surfaces\n\n- Player: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\n- Game Master: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\n- Sections: `journal`, `chart`, `treasures`, `quests`, `log`, `finale`\n- Navigation: `?section=<id>` with pushState/popstate restoration, shared desktop/mobile state, and one SSE connection\n- Persistent current objective, compact preferences, player-initiated fullscreen, artifact inspection, chapter index, accessible map list, and event-derived log are implemented\n\n## Architecture\n\n- Next.js App Router, Prisma SQLite/MySQL, database-backed sessions, ordered domain events/SSE, central ceremony queue\n- Public snapshots are server allowlist projections; raw event payloads never reach the player stream\n- New models: `MapRoute`, `ViewedContent`\n- Extended models: campaign finale shell; chapter safe teasers/cross-links; artifact release/assembly state; map safe labels/reveal metadata; optional quest rewards/links; journal annotations; companion preferences\n- New event families: route reveal, artifact silhouette/connection, quest completion, annotations/log entries, finale tease/requirements\n- Ship's Log is derived by `src/domain/ships-log.ts`, not stored as duplicate display history\n- Unseen state is cross-device per player access identity; ceremony playback remains device-local\n\n## Development presets\n\nRun `npm run db:preset -- <preset>` to reset the development campaign. Supported values:\n\n- `awaiting-first-release` (default seed)\n- `active-chapter`\n- `mid-voyage`\n- `artifact-award-ready`\n- `side-quest-active`\n- `nearly-complete-shell`\n- `all-empty`\n- `long-log`\n- `mobile-stress-test`\n\nPresets are CLI-only, safe to repeat, development-content-only, and not exposed to players or production UI.\n\n## Commands\n\n- Start everything: `npm run dev:full`\n- Stop: `npm run dev:stop`\n- Complete validation: `npm run validate`\n- SQLite generate/migrate/seed: `npm run db:generate && npm run db:migrate && npm run db:seed`\n- MySQL companion migration: apply `db:migrate:mysql:init` then `db:migrate:mysql:companion`\n\nLocal development defaults come from `.env.example`: player phrase `development-moonwake`; GM `kato` / `development-captain-only`. Never use them outside an isolated local development database.\n\n## Validation state\n\n- Strict TypeScript: passing\n- Unit tests: 13 passing across 5 files\n- ESLint: passing\n- Production build: passing\n- SQLite migrations: clean application of init + companion migration\n- `mid-voyage` fixture integrity: 12 contiguous events, 12 snapshots, 12 audits\n- In-app browser: all six sections, deep-link history, artifact dialog, sealed finale, 1440/1920 desktop and 390 mobile states inspected with zero horizontal overflow and no console warnings\n- Chromium/WebKit automated acceptance and clean-clone validation are part of the final task gate; consult the ending task report for exact counts/artifacts\n\n## Known limitations and next phase\n\n- Real story, riddles, locations, photographs, personal memories, romantic messages, production tokens, and finale content remain blocked until the repository is private.\n- Finale states beyond `REQUIREMENTS_PARTIAL` and real assembly are intentionally unavailable.\n- SSE fan-out is process-local; add Redis/equivalent before multi-instance deployment.\n- Initial log projection is capped at 250 entries; add cursor pagination for larger production histories.\n- Exercise the parallel MySQL migration against MySQL 8 in CI.\n- Recommended Phase 3: make the repository private, establish the Creative Bible/content pipeline, then author final story through versioned private content without changing the shell architecture.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\decisions\\0001-use-nextjs-app-router.md =====\n\r\n# ADR 0001: Next.js App Router\n\nDate: 2026-07-16 Â· Status: accepted\n\nContext: the companion needs server-protected content, responsive React UI, and Debian self-hosting. Decision: Next.js App Router with strict TypeScript and Node runtime. Alternatives: SPA/API split and Remix. Consequences: cohesive authorization/data boundaries and one deployable service, with framework runtime discipline required.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\decisions\\0002-use-server-sent-events.md =====\n\r\n# ADR 0002: Server-Sent Events\n\nDate: 2026-07-16 Â· Status: accepted\n\nContext: progression is server-to-player and ordered. Decision: SSE with event IDs, heartbeat, reconnect, Last-Event-ID/query recovery, and snapshot reconciliation. Alternatives: WebSockets and polling. Consequences: simpler proxy/runtime behavior; multi-instance fan-out later needs shared pub/sub.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\decisions\\0003-use-event-driven-story-state.md =====\n\r\n# ADR 0003: Event-driven story state\n\nDate: 2026-07-16 Â· Status: accepted\n\nContext: refresh, duplicate delivery, close actions, replay, and undo must be safe. Decision: transactional domain state plus immutable sequenced events, snapshots, audit records, and save states. Alternatives: chapter-number mutation and client-only state. Consequences: reliable reconciliation and history at the cost of more explicit transaction code.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\deployment.md =====\n\r\n# Deployment\n\nTarget: Debian, Node 24, MySQL 8, NGINX, HTTPS, and systemd. Do not deploy from development seed credentials.\n\n1. Create MySQL database/user and set `DATABASE_URL=mysql://...`.\n2. For a new database run `npm ci`, `npm run db:generate:mysql`, `npm run db:migrate:mysql:init`, then `npm run build`. Record future MySQL migrations under `prisma/mysql-migrations` before production use.\n3. Install `deploy/forever-treasure.service`, place secrets in `/etc/forever-treasure.env`, and proxy with `deploy/nginx.conf`.\n4. Send application logs to journald; retain NGINX access/error logs under `/var/log/nginx`.\n\nBack up with daily encrypted `mysqldump --single-transaction`, retain seven daily/four weekly copies off-host, and test quarterly restore into a separate database. Restore by stopping the service, importing a verified dump, applying pending migrations, and starting/reconciling. Generate secrets with `openssl rand -base64 48`.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\design-system.md =====\n\r\n# Design system\n\n## Companion sections\n\nThe shared ocean-dark, parchment, brass, moonlight, and muted-crimson tokens remain authoritative. Journal uses ink/page hierarchy; Chart uses fog and cartographic marks; Altar uses wood/brass placement; Ledger uses secret-note rhythm; Log uses an engraved timeline; Finale uses a dormant celestial mechanism. All retain the same focus ring, corner language, shadows, texture ceiling, typography scale, and motion tokens.\n\nTokens live in `src/app/globals.css`: abyss navy `#061316`, ocean green `#123b3a`, parchment `#d9c69a`, antique brass `#b89551`, crimson `#782f35`, and moonlit teal `#79d2c3`. Georgia/system serif keeps story text readable; Arial is reserved for small operational labels. Paper grain, edge wear, chart lines, brass frames, restrained glow, and vignette are procedural CSSâ€”no copied game assets. Focus is a 3px warm-gold outline. Layers reserve 20â€“35 for persistent chrome, 60 for journal opening, 70 for ceremony controls, and 90 for GM confirmations.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\finale-shell.md =====\n\r\n# Finale shell\n\nThe schema supports `HIDDEN`, `TEASED`, `SEALED`, `REQUIREMENTS_PARTIAL`, `READY`, `UNLOCKED`, and `COMPLETE`, but Phase 2 uses only the first four. Public requirements are generic symbolic counters. Optional stars are marked optional and do not block the main voyage.\n\nNo real finale copy, object, dependency, destination, or unlock sequence is present. `READY`, `UNLOCKED`, and `COMPLETE` remain structural future states and must not be activated until a later authorized phase.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\game-master-controls.md =====\n\r\n# Game Master controls\n\n## Phase 2 controls\n\nAuthenticated progression supports map/route reveal, artifact award/silhouette/connection, side-quest discover/update/complete, journal annotation, player-facing log entry, finale tease, and generic finale requirement update. Each call requires session authentication, CSRF, explicit confirmation, a transaction, a domain event, a campaign snapshot, and an audit row. Development presets are CLI-only and never appear in the player or production UI.\n\nPrepare, release, solve, artifact award, map reveal, pause, resume, and undo all use select â†’ impact preview â†’ confirmation â†’ atomic execution â†’ event receipt. The release transaction activates the chapter and reveals its intentionally imprecise map marker. Undo restores the latest save-state and emits `STATE_REVERTED` so players reconcile. Preview Reveal and Replay Reveal Locally are represented by the safe player replay control in this slice; a dedicated isolated GM preview timeline is future work.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\journal-system.md =====\n\r\n# Journal system\n\nChapters use `LOCKED`, `TEASER`, `READY`, `REVEALING`, `ACTIVE`, `SOLVED`, and `COMPLETE`. Locked/ready chapters serialize only ordinal, state, and an explicitly safe teaser. Narrative, objective, clue, released hints, annotations, and cross-links serialize only for readable states.\n\nHints are ordered records with a nullable release time. The server omits unreleased hints. Viewed state uses the player access identity and stable content keys, so it reconciles across devices that share the invitation identity. Annotations are released `JournalEntry` records and never include GM audit metadata.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\known-limitations.md =====\n\r\n# Known limitations\n\n## Phase 2 boundaries\n\n- The repository is public; final story and surprise content is intentionally blocked.\n- Finale unlock/assembly is structural only and cannot enter `READY` or later states through Phase 2 controls.\n- SSE fan-out remains process-local and needs Redis or equivalent before multi-instance production deployment.\n- Initial Ship's Log projection is capped at 250 entries; cursor pagination is future scale work.\n- Production MySQL migration must be exercised against an actual MySQL 8 integration environment.\n- Final art/audio, real content authoring, and production deployment belong to later phases.\n\n- SSE fan-out is process-local; database replay is durable, but multi-instance production needs Redis/pub-sub.\n- Dedicated GM-only preview timeline, explicit replay targeting, and richer player presence are not yet implemented.\n- The full mutation workflow runs once in Chromium to avoid cross-project database contention; privacy, route protection, accessibility, and mobile behavior also run in WebKit.\n- Windows LAN access is opt-in and may still require a firewall rule or network-policy change; localhost is the safe default.\n- SQLite and MySQL schemas are intentionally parallel; schema edits must update both and add SQL to the connector-specific migration histories until a generation workflow is added.\n- Final story content, private media, polished licensed audio, production monitoring, and deployment remain out of scope.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\local-development.md =====\n\r\n# Local development\n\n## One-command Windows startup\n\nDouble-click `Start Forever Treasure Dev.cmd` or run `npm run dev:full`. The idempotent launcher:\n\n1. Finds Node/npm and explains what is missing when neither a normal installation nor the bundled Codex runtime is available.\n2. Creates `.env` only when absent; existing local configuration is preserved and validated.\n3. Mirrors network-share checkouts to `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\ForeverTreasureCompanion\\development` while keeping dependencies, databases, logs, and generated artifacts out of Git.\n4. Runs `npm ci` only when `package-lock.json` changed or dependencies are missing.\n5. Generates Prisma, applies the versioned SQLite migration, and runs the idempotent development seed.\n6. Refuses to take an occupied port, starts the server as a hidden recorded process, and waits for an HTTP health response.\n7. Prints the player and GM URLs, disposable credentials, and stop command.\n\nRun `npm run dev:stop` before switching branches or replacing the runtime. Shutdown verifies the recorded PID is the expected Next.js development process before stopping it.\n\n## URLs and credentials\n\n- Player URL: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\n- Invitation phrase: `development-moonwake`\n- GM URL: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\n- GM username/password: `kato` / `development-captain-only`\n\nThe launcher-generated `.env` is ignored. Change these values for any shared environment. Never reuse them for deployment.\n\n## LAN opt-in\n\n`powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File scripts/start-dev.ps1 -Lan` binds Next.js to `0.0.0.0` and prints the best detected IPv4 URLs. LAN mode is intentionally not the one-click default because it exposes a development server and disposable credentials to the local network. If another device cannot connect, allow the selected TCP port through Windows Firewall and confirm that corporate Wi-Fi/client-isolation policy permits peer traffic.\n\n## Portable fallback\n\nOn macOS/Linux or when automation is unavailable:\n\n```bash\nnpm ci\ncp .env.example .env\nnpm run db:generate\nnpm run db:migrate\nnpm run db:seed\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nUse Node 24 and npm 11.9.0; `.nvmrc`, `.node-version`, `packageManager`, and `package-lock.json` define the reproducible toolchain.\n\n## Clean-clone continuation\n\nAn authorized session on another computer needs only Git, Node/npm, and this repository. Clone `main`, copy `.env.example` or use the Windows launcher, then run `npm run validate`. No database, generated Prisma client, Playwright browser state, artifact, or machine path is required from the originating computer.\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\n- **Port 3000 is occupied:** stop that process or run `scripts/start-dev.ps1 -Port 3001`. The launcher never kills an unknown listener.\n- **Existing `.env` is incomplete:** the launcher preserves it and identifies each missing key. Add the key; it will not overwrite local secrets.\n- **Startup fails:** inspect `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\ForeverTreasureCompanion\\development\\.forever\\logs`.\n- **Database is disposable:** stop the app, remove the ignored local `prisma/dev.db`, and rerun startup. For validation, `npm run validate` always rebuilds `validation.db` from migrations.\n- **Browser binaries are missing:** `npm run validate` installs pinned Chromium and WebKit builds automatically.\n- **UNC/network checkout is slow:** the first mirror/install is expected to take longer; subsequent runs retain the dependency cache and only synchronize project files.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\player-companion-information-architecture.md =====\n\r\n# Player Companion information architecture\n\nThe companion has six URL-addressable sections: `journal`, `chart`, `treasures`, `quests`, `log`, and `finale`. The selected section is stored in the `section` query parameter so refresh, deep links, back/forward navigation, and mobile/desktop handoff share one model. A persistent objective links back to the active journal chapter.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  O[Current objective] --> J[Journal]\n  J <--> C[Voyage chart]\n  J <--> T[Treasure altar]\n  J <--> Q[Side-quest ledger]\n  C <--> Q\n  T --> F[Sealed finale]\n  J --> L[Ship's log]\n  C --> L\n  T --> L\n  Q --> L\n  L --> J\n  L --> C\n  L --> T\n  L --> Q\n  L --> F\n```\n\nAll sections are projections of one authenticated public snapshot and one ordered SSE stream. Navigation never fetches hidden content and never starts a second polling loop.\n\nDesktop uses an artifact-like section rail and layered workspace. iPhone uses the same semantic navigation as a safe-area-aware bottom strip and one-column content. Locked links may navigate only to a safe locked state; they never cause protected payloads to be requested.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\responsive-behavior.md =====\n\r\n# Responsive behavior\n\n## Complete shell\n\nDesktop uses the available monitor as a layered story workspace with persistent one-action navigation and compact objective access. Mobile switches to a single content column, labeled bottom navigation, safe-area padding, separate section views, and a compact return-to-clue control. The map always has a text alternative. Artifact inspection becomes a full-screen sheet on narrow screens. Fullscreen mode removes secondary chrome without removing navigation or the objective.\n\nAbove 1100px, chart, open journal, and relic display form a three-region physical workspace. At 1100px and below, bottom navigation exposes one view at a time. At 650px, the journal becomes one vertically readable parchment sheet with safe-area-aware bottom controls. Short phone landscape hides the spine/left sheet and uses a compact single-page composition. Capability choices use CSS width, height, orientation, hover-independent controls, safe-area insets, and `prefers-reduced-motion`; no user-agent sniffing is used.\n\nValidated viewports: 1920Ã—1080, 1440Ã—900, 390Ã—844, 430Ã—932, and 844Ã—390. 2560Ã—1440 was layout-reviewed through the same capped workspace rules; automated retained capture remains next-pass work.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\security.md =====\n\r\n# Security\n\n## Companion release boundary\n\nSerialization is allowlist-based. Locked chapters omit narrative/objective/clues; unreleased hints and annotations are absent; rumored map locations omit coordinates/internal regions; unknown artifacts omit names/descriptions; hidden quests are absent and rumors expose only safe teasers; hidden finale details are never modeled in public content. SSE payloads pass through the same explicit allowlist and never stream raw stored payloads. Player routes require a campaign-bound, unexpired access identity and expose no mutation endpoint.\n\nThe repository is public as of 2026-07-16. Real story, surprise, romantic, location, photographic, token, and finale content is prohibited until visibility is verified private.\n\nPlayer access uses a bcrypt-verified campaign code and strict HTTP-only cookie. GM login uses bcrypt, database sessions, secure/strict cookies, a per-session CSRF token, generic failures, and a five-failure/15-minute fingerprint limit. Zod validates mutation input. Admin actions are audited. Logs redact passwords, access codes, cookies, and authorization headers. Player snapshots omit all chapter content until the chapter is released; tests and manual DOM/network inspection verify the gate contains no clue text.\n\nProduction must set a random 32+ byte session secret, unique GM password and player code, HTTPS, reverse-proxy rate controls, database least privilege, and private repository visibility before real surprise content is committed.\n\nThe repository was still treated as public during the automated-demo milestone. Only generic development clues, locations, messages, and credentials are committed; validation performs a deliberate diff/secret review before publication. LAN development is an explicit opt-in because binding to `0.0.0.0` exposes the disposable demo surface to peers allowed by the host firewall.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\ships-log.md =====\n\r\n# Ship's Log\n\nThe Ship's Log is derived from ordered `ProgressEvent` records by `src/domain/ships-log.ts`. The transformation owns player-facing title, summary, symbol, importance, destination section, and safe target key. Unknown/internal events return no entry and never crash rendering.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  G[Authenticated GM action] --> E[Domain event]\n  E --> S[Campaign snapshot]\n  E --> A[Audit record]\n  E --> T[Player log transformer]\n  T --> L[Atmospheric log entry]\n  E --> SSE[Ordered SSE]\n  SSE --> L\n```\n\nRaw payloads, actor IDs, audit records, credentials, and internal notes are never log fields. The snapshot caps the initial log projection at 250 entries; later phases may add cursor pagination beyond that boundary.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\side-quest-system.md =====\n\r\n# Side-quest system\n\nOptional mysteries use `HIDDEN`, `RUMORED`, `DISCOVERED`, `ACTIVE`, `PARTIALLY_COMPLETE`, `COMPLETE`, `MISSED`, and `ARCHIVED`. Hidden records are omitted. Rumored records expose only a safe teaser. Discovered and later records may expose title, premise, ordered objectives, safe reward, and released cross-links.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  H[HIDDEN] --> R[RUMORED]\n  R --> D[DISCOVERED]\n  D --> A[ACTIVE]\n  A --> P[PARTIALLY_COMPLETE]\n  P --> C[COMPLETE]\n  A -. gentle expiry .-> M[MISSED]\n  C --> X[ARCHIVED]\n```\n\nSide quests never block the main chapter state machine. Rewards are persisted, idempotent domain outcomes and may enrichâ€”not gateâ€”the future finale.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\story-state-machine.md =====\n\r\n# Story state machine\n\n## Companion sub-state\n\nChapter transitions remain authoritative. Map, route, artifact, side-quest, and finale state changes are separate domain events projected into the same campaign snapshot. They never bypass chapter visibility. New event types include route reveal, artifact silhouette/connection, quest completion, annotation/log arrival, and finale tease/requirement changes. Undo captures every mutable companion subsystem before applying a progression action.\n\n```mermaid\nstateDiagram-v2\n  LOCKED --> TEASER\n  LOCKED --> READY\n  TEASER --> READY\n  READY --> REVEALING\n  REVEALING --> ACTIVE\n  ACTIVE --> SOLVED\n  SOLVED --> COMPLETE\n  READY --> LOCKED: undo\n  ACTIVE --> READY: undo\n  SOLVED --> ACTIVE: undo\n```\n\nThe schema stores state and ordered immutable events rather than a chapter number alone. The vertical slice releases READY directly to durable ACTIVE in one transaction so animation failure never blocks truth; REVEALING is a client ceremony phase and remains available for future server acknowledgements. Every mutation saves a restore point, increments a campaign sequence, creates an event/snapshot/audit record, then publishes after commit. Event IDs plus device ceremony acknowledgements provide idempotency.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\testing.md =====\n\r\n# Testing\n\n## Phase 2 coverage\n\nUnit coverage includes event reconciliation, event-to-log transformation, malformed/unknown event fallback, and event payload allowlisting. Database verification checks the five-chapter, five-location, two-route, five-artifact, and four-quest development fixture structure plus contiguous event/snapshot/audit invariants. Presets are reset with `npm run db:preset -- <name>`; default `npm run db:seed` retains the sealed acceptance start.\n\nRun the complete gate with:\n\n```powershell\nnpm run validate\n```\n\nThe command creates a clean local runtime and disposable `validation.db`, then runs, in order:\n\n1. Prisma generation, migration deployment, idempotent seed, and baseline database invariants.\n2. Prettier check, ESLint, and strict TypeScript.\n3. Six Vitest state-machine/ceremony tests.\n4. Pinned Chromium and WebKit installation.\n5. Five cross-browser access/security/accessibility checks and one complete Chromium GM/player mutation workflow.\n6. Post-acceptance database verification for contiguous events, matching snapshots/audits, deduplication, persistence, and undo state.\n7. Optimized Next.js production build.\n8. Two consecutive production start/health/stop cycles on port 3200.\n\nThe mutation workflow covers invalid/correct player access, unauthenticated/wrong/correct/rate-limited GM access, cookie isolation, prepare-without-disclosure, live SSE release, 5â€“9 second ceremony timing, stage captures, skip/replay, mute and reduced motion, refresh persistence, duplicate rejection, artifact/map persistence, solve/undo, offline pause/resume reconciliation, heartbeat, accessibility scans, and 2560/1920/1440/430/390/landscape viewports.\n\nReports, traces, screenshots, and production logs are ignored and written to `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\ForeverTreasureCompanion\\validation\\artifacts\\validation` for network-share checkouts, or `artifacts/validation` for local checkouts. A successful run currently reports 6 unit tests, 5 browser tests passing, 1 intentionally skipped cross-browser duplicate of the mutation workflow, 7 persisted events/audits, a successful build, and a successful restart proof.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\theatrical-event-system.md =====\n\r\n# Theatrical event system\n\n## Phase 2 ceremonies\n\nMeaningful map, artifact, quest, log, objective, and finale events reuse the ordered ceremony queue. Routine section navigation stays fast. Every event has a final-state snapshot, reduced-motion path, skip path, and stable event identity; refresh does not replay an acknowledged ceremony. See `docs/cinematic-system.md` for reusable motion primitives.\n\n```mermaid\nsequenceDiagram\n  participant GM\n  participant Server\n  participant Player\n  GM->>Server: Confirm release\n  Server->>Server: Transaction + event sequence\n  Server-->>Player: SSE CHAPTER_RELEASED\n  Player->>Player: Omen â†’ attention â†’ seal â†’ parchment\n  Player->>Player: Heading â†’ story â†’ objective â†’ riddle â†’ map\n  Player->>Server: Ceremony viewed acknowledgement\n```\n\n`useCeremony` owns one sorted queue and AbortController; components contain no independent timeout chains. Default timing totals about 6.3 seconds. Reduced motion uses attention, grouped ink stages, map, and active state with 120ms caps. Skip aborts presentation but still reconciles the authoritative snapshot. Replay creates a local synthetic event ID and never mutates progression. Audio is initialized only by â€œOpen the journalâ€; the seal tone is procedural and low-volume.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\unseen-content.md =====\n\r\n# Unseen content\n\n`ViewedContent` stores `(playerAccessId, contentType, contentKey)` uniquely. This makes acknowledgements idempotent and persistent across refreshes and devices using the same player access identity. Ceremony playback remains device-specific in `ViewedCeremony` because replay expectations are local.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  E[Released domain event] --> P[Public snapshot projection]\n  P --> U[Unseen indicator]\n  U --> V[Meaningful section view]\n  V --> API[Viewed-content API]\n  API --> DB[(ViewedContent)]\n  DB --> R[Reconciled snapshot]\n  R --> C[Indicator cleared]\n```\n\nContent types are chapter, hint, annotation, map, route, artifact, quest, log, and finale. Indicators use text/screen-reader meaning in addition to visual marks.\n\r\n\n===== C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt\\docs\\voyage-chart.md =====\n\r\n# Voyage Chart\n\nThe chart is a data-driven fictional narrative map. `MapLocation` stores internal labels, safe labels, reveal state, exactness, display coordinates, chapter/quest links, and completion. Coordinates and internal region details serialize only after an exact reveal. Rumored locations receive a safe label without coordinates.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  U[UNKNOWN] --> R[RUMORED]\n  R --> V[REVEALED]\n  V --> A[ACTIVE_DESTINATION]\n  A --> I[VISITED]\n  I --> C[COMPLETED]\n  V --> S[SIDE_QUEST]\n```\n\n`MapRoute` stores ordered endpoint keys and reveal state. A route is public only when it and both endpoints are revealed. The visual chart must also expose an equivalent keyboard-accessible location list.\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

FEATURE HANDOFF ---\r\n# Codex handoff\n\n- Canonical repository: `Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT`\n- Phase 3 branch: `feature/game-master-command-center`; original base `70bb654b78a84df15dba8d0f9ce5b3fd5782181d`\n- Integration base: Phase 2 merged `origin/main` at `ede3764`; reconciled by a normal merge, preserving both histories\n- Status: expanded Command Center plus complete Phase 2 Player Companion; not integrated back into `main`\n- Command Center routes: `/quartermaster` plus `/chapters`, `/hints`, `/voyage`, `/artifacts`, `/quests`, `/journal`, `/events`, `/player-view`, `/recovery`, `/audit`, `/diagnostics`\n- Authentication: GM bcrypt/database session and CSRF; player sessions cannot call admin APIs\n- Commands: prepare/release/solve/complete chapter, prepare/release hint, reveal map, award artifact, discover/advance quest, release journal entry, pause/resume, undo, reconciliation\n- Migration: Phase 2 `20260716223000_player_companion_shell`, then Phase 3 `20260716233000_game_master_command_center`; connector-parity MySQL SQL exists\n- Development data: Phase 2 multi-state presets plus Phase 3 prepared-action and audit fixtures\n- Startup: `npm run dev:full`; validation: `npm run validate`; stop: `npm run dev:stop`\n- Known limitations: no background scheduler, multi-event rollback, automated presence cleanup, Redis fan-out, real finale/story/private media, or deployment\n- Latest fully validated implementation commit: `8174f28` (16 unit, 9 executed browser, build, and two restart proofs)\n- Draft pull request: `https://github.com/Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT/pull/1`\n- Recommended next task: controlled integration review and production-readiness work; do not merge the PR without the post-merge checklist\n\n--- FEATURE LIMITATIONS ---\r\n# Known limitations\n\n## Phase 2 boundaries\n\n- The repository is public; final story and surprise content is intentionally blocked.\n- Finale unlock/assembly is structural only and cannot enter `READY` or later states through Phase 2 controls.\n- SSE fan-out remains process-local and needs Redis or equivalent before multi-instance production deployment.\n- Initial Ship's Log projection is capped at 250 entries; cursor pagination is future scale work.\n- Production MySQL migration must be exercised against an actual MySQL 8 integration environment.\n- Final art/audio, real content authoring, and production deployment belong to later phases.\n\n- SSE fan-out is process-local; database replay is durable, but multi-instance production needs Redis/pub-sub.\n- The Phase 3 preview is a sanitized state projection rather than a pixel-identical embedded Player Companion ceremony timeline; explicit replay targeting is not implemented.\n- The full mutation workflow runs once in Chromium to avoid cross-project database contention; privacy, route protection, accessibility, and mobile behavior also run in WebKit.\n- Windows LAN access is opt-in and may still require a firewall rule or network-policy change; localhost is the safe default.\n- SQLite and MySQL schemas are intentionally parallel; schema edits must update both and add SQL to the connector-specific migration histories until a generation workflow is added.\n- Final story content, private media, polished licensed audio, production monitoring, and deployment remain out of scope.\n- Scheduling stores a future timestamp and stale-state evidence but has no background execution worker; scheduled actions require Game Master review and release.\n- Recovery exposes only the latest safe single-step reversal. Full dependency-graph multi-event rollback is intentionally unavailable.\n- Presence is durable per device but cleanup is documented rather than automated; production should expire records older than 30 days.\n\n--- FEATURE INTEGRATION ---\r\n# Phase 2 and Phase 3 integration plan\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart TD\n  A[Integrate Phase 2 player contracts] --> B[Resolve event union additively]\n  B --> C[Combine Prisma models and migrations]\n  C --> D[Retain Phase 2 visibility projection]\n  D --> E[Adapt presence to final player routes]\n  E --> F[Clean migration + two-browser suite]\n```\n\nThis branch began on pre-Phase-2 `main` at `70bb654` and now includes Phase 2 `main` through `ede3764` via a normal merge commit. Phase 2 remains authoritative for player rendering/visibility; Phase 3 remains authoritative for administrative intent, presence, execution, staging, and audit correlation.\n\nMigration order: Phase 1 init, Phase 2 additive migration, then Phase 3 command-center migration. Reconciliation tests cover older snapshots, hidden content, hint/artifact/map/quest delivery, route presence, acknowledgement, SSE reconnect, preview nonmutation, stale commands, and reversal.\n\n--- FEATURE PARALLEL ---\r\n# Phase 3 parallel-development record\n\n- Base and starting commit: `70bb654b78a84df15dba8d0f9ce5b3fd5782181d`\n- Branch: `feature/game-master-command-center`\n- Base branch: `origin/main`\n- Worktree: isolated from the dirty Phase 2 checkout\n- Reconciliation: Phase 2 landed on `origin/main` at `ede3764`; that updated main was merged into this feature branch after the initial Phase 3 validation. Phase 2 player visibility/rendering and seed presets were retained, with Phase 3 administrative relations, presence lifecycle, CSS, and fixtures applied additively.\n\n## Shared contract review\n\nExisting behavior uses `src/domain/story.ts` for seven chapter states and ordered player events, `src/lib/snapshot.ts` for the player-safe projection, and `src/server/progression.ts` as the Phase 1 transaction boundary. Phase 3 needs administrative commands, staging, preview, idempotency, presence, and audit correlation. Phase 2 is likely to touch `story.ts`, both Prisma schemas, seed data, the player component, and the snapshot.\n\nCompatibility strategy: administrative schemas live in `src/domain/admin.ts`; new tables and audit fields are additive; existing fields and endpoints remain; presence is additive to legacy `lastSeenAt`; the public snapshot shape is unchanged; player event additions use optional version-1 payloads. Preview clones the sanitized snapshot and never writes.\n\nShared files modified: `src/domain/story.ts`, `src/server/progression.ts`, `src/components/player/PlayerExperience.tsx`, both Prisma schemas, `prisma/seed.ts`, and appended Command Center CSS. New events are `HINT_PREPARED`, `MAP_REVEAL_PREPARED`, `ARTIFACT_AWARD_PREPARED`, `SIDE_QUEST_UPDATE_PREPARED`, `PLAYER_RECONCILIATION_REQUESTED`, and `NARRATIVE_MESSAGE_RELEASED`.\n\nNew APIs: `POST /api/gm/commands`, `POST /api/gm/preview`, `POST /api/gm/staging`, and `POST /api/player/[campaignSlug]/presence`. Admin routes require a GM session; writes also require CSRF. Commands validate expected sequence and idempotency key.\n\nResolved conflicts were Phase 2 snapshot/event additions, schema relations on `PlayerAccess`, seed fixtures, the player lifecycle, global styles, Game Master UI, verification rules, and handoff docs. Phase 2 visibility and Player Companion stayed authoritative; Phase 3 Command Center stayed authoritative. Post-merge validation regenerates both clients, migrates clean SQLite, and runs unit, E2E, accessibility, build, two-context SSE, preview-nonmutation, stale-command, and leakage checks.\n", "type": "input_text"}]
